Slowly pour in the chicken broth while stirring, scraping the bottom of the pot with the spoon to loosen any stuck on flour or vegetables.

Add the milk, salt, and black pepper. Bring to a simmer. Reduce the heat just high enough to maintain a low simmer, low to medium-low. (Try to keep it from boiling hard.) Partially cover and continue simmering until the potatoes are very tender, about 40 minutes. For a thicker and less chunky soup, mash the potatoes with the back of a spoon or a potato masher to your desired consistency. Stir in the cream and sour cream. Taste and add more salt and pepper, if desired. Serve immediately topped with sour cream, cheese, chives, and the cooked bacon.
notes: Don't toss the bacon drippings! After you cook the bacon, be sure to save the drippings to cook the onion and garlic in. It adds a lot of great flavor to the soup. If you don't want to use bacon in the soup, skip this step and melt 4 tablespoons of butter in the pot before adding the veggies.
Create a smooth texture. If you prefer a smooth soup texture rather than chunky, use an immersion blender to blend the soup to your desired consistency once it's cooked.

Make it gluten-free. Omit the flour and instead whisk in one tablespoon of cornstarch into the milk. Then add the milk to the recipe as outlined. Or, you can substitute your favorite brand of 1-to-1 gluten-free flour. Either option will make the soup just as thick!

Add extra toppings. Load it up! Try adding seasonings like fresh thyme, red chili flakes, and extra black pepper, or add a dash of hot sauce. Use a mix of shredded cheeses like cheddar and smoked Gouda, or try shredded goat cheese. Or stick to the classics- sour cream, chives or green onions, and cheddar cheese!

Options variations. Sneak in some extra veggies by adding chopped cabbage, spinach, broccoli, cauliflower, carrots, corn, peas, or leeks when you add the potatoes to the pot. Additionally, you can add a blend of seasonings, such as Cajun or Italian seasoning mix, for different flavors.
title: Potato Soup

author: Amanda Cooks & Styles
description: Super simple homemade breakfast potato recipe made with fresh potatoes, herbs and seasonings. Serve with fresh cooked eggs, toast, in a breakfast burrito and more.
ingredients: 1 tablespoon Chopped Parsley, fresh, or 1/2 teaspoon dried
instructions: Cut all potatoes into small 1/2-inch cubes.
Heat skillet to medium high heat and add butter and oil.

Add chopped potatoes to the hot skillet and toss around to fully coat in butter/oil combination. Sprinkle potatoes with seasonings except for the parsley. Cover to let potatoes lightly steam for about 3-4 minutes.
Remove lid and stir potatoes. Continue to cook potatoes with lid off, turning potatoes around with spatula to ensure an even cook on all sides.
When potatoes are done they will have a light crisp on the outside, soft on the inside.
Sprinkle potatoes with fresh parsley and serve.
title: Easy Breakfast Potatoes

author: Robert Carr
description: A reverse-seared thick-cut Wagyu-style ribeye using a measured salt dry brine, low oven cook, final dry pat, and hard stainless steel pan sear with an optional brief garlic-thyme butter baste.
ingredients: 1 thick-cut ribeye steak, about 1 pound and at least 1 1/2 inches thick
3.4 grams kosher salt, about 0.75% of the steak weight
1/2 teaspoon black pepper, freshly ground, added after searing or lightly before oven step
1 to 2 teaspoons avocado oil, or another neutral high-heat oil
1 pinch flaky salt, optional, for finishing
instructions: Thaw steak fully in the refrigerator if frozen.
Weigh the steak in grams if possible.
Calculate kosher salt as 0.75% of the steak weight. Use the formula: steak weight in grams multiplied by 0.0075 equals salt in grams.
For a 1 pound steak, use about 3.4 grams kosher salt total for the top, bottom, edges, and fat cap.
Remove steak from packaging and pat very dry on all sides with paper towels.
Season steak evenly on all sides with the measured kosher salt only.
Place a wire rack over a baking sheet or plate, then place the steak on the wire rack uncovered.
Refrigerate uncovered for 4 to 8 hours, or up to 12 hours if using the conservative 0.75% salt amount. This dry-brines the steak and dries the surface for a better crust.
Remove steak from the refrigerator 30 to 45 minutes before cooking.
Pat steak dry again if any surface moisture appears.
Preheat oven to 225°F.
Place steak on a wire rack over a baking sheet and cook in the oven until the center reaches 115°F to 118°F for classic medium-rare, or 118°F to 120°F for medium-rare leaning medium.

Begin checking the internal temperature around 20 minutes using an instant-read thermometer inserted into the center from the side. Continue checking every 5 minutes, then every 2 to 3 minutes once close to the target temperature.
Remove steak from oven and rest for 5 to 10 minutes on a wire rack or cutting board while preheating the stainless steel pan.
Preheat a stainless steel pan on induction level 6.5 to 7 until a few drops of water bead and glide across the pan.
Just before searing, pat the steak very dry on all sides again.
Wipe out any water from the pan, add avocado oil, and heat until shimmering.
If the steak has a pronounced fat cap, hold it with tongs and render the fat cap for 30 to 60 seconds first.
Place steak in the pan and sear the broad sides for 45 to 75 seconds per side, pressing gently to ensure good pan contact.
Sear any remaining edges using tongs until browned.
Reduce heat slightly, add butter, smashed garlic, and thyme if using, then baste briefly for 20 to 30 seconds so the butter flavors the crust without overwhelming the beef.
Remove steak from pan and rest for 5 minutes on a wire rack or cutting board.
Slice against the grain.
Finish with flaky salt and black pepper if desired.
notes: This method works best with a steak at least 1 1/2 inches thick, and even better around 1 3/4 to 2 inches thick.
Best target for this steak is medium-rare leaning medium, around 132°F to 137°F final internal temperature.
For a fattier Wagyu-style ribeye, avoid rare because the fat may stay waxy instead of softening and rendering.
Doneness targets: for medium-rare, pull from the oven at 115°F to 118°F and finish around 130°F to 135°F after searing and resting.
Doneness targets: for medium-rare leaning medium, pull from the oven at 118°F to 120°F and finish around 132°F to 137°F after searing and resting.
Doneness targets: for medium, pull from the oven at 122°F to 125°F and finish around 138°F to 145°F after searing and resting.
Salt before the refrigerator dry brine. Add black pepper after searing or lightly before the oven step because pepper can burn during a hard sear.
Use 0.75% salt by steak weight for a conservative dry brine. Formula: steak weight in grams multiplied by 0.0075 equals salt in grams.
For this 1 pound steak, 0.75% salt is about 3.4 grams total.
A lighter 0.5% salt level would be about 2.27 grams for this steak. A normal 1.0% level would be about 4.54 grams.
If using volume instead of weight, 3.4 grams is roughly 1 heaping teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt or about 2/3 to 3/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt.
For best texture, dry brine this steak for 4 to 8 hours. Up to 12 hours is reasonable with the conservative 0.75% salt amount.
Avoid pushing the dry brine much past 12 hours for this steak unless using a very light salt level, because longer or heavier dry brines can make the outer texture seem slightly cured.
If short on time, even 1 hour uncovered in the fridge helps. If there is no time, salt right before cooking and pat the steak extremely dry.
The salt dry brine seasons the steak more deeply. Salt first draws out a little moisture, dissolves, then gets pulled back into the meat over time.
The wire rack matters because it lets air circulate around the steak. A plate traps moisture underneath and keeps the bottom wet.
The uncovered refrigerator step dries the surface. A drier surface browns faster and forms a better crust.
The final dry pat before searing is important because moisture from the oven step can soften the surface and slow browning.
Do not braise this steak. Use the oven only for the low-temperature reverse sear step, then finish in the stainless steel pan.
For the cleanest beef flavor, skip the butter baste or keep it very brief. For a steakhouse finish, use the butter, garlic, and thyme for only the last 20 to 30 seconds.
A laser thermometer is less reliable on shiny stainless steel; the water bead test and steak internal temperature are more useful.
If the pan smokes violently before the steak goes in, lower the induction level slightly.
Leftovers reheat best gently. Warm leftover steak in a 250°F oven on a wire rack over a baking sheet until the center reaches about 100°F to 110°F.
Do not reheat leftover steak back to 130°F or higher unless you are okay with it becoming more cooked than the original steak.
If desired, briefly touch up the crust after reheating by searing the cut sides in a hot pan for 10 to 20 seconds per side.
Leftover steak is also good cold or room temperature, sliced thin with flaky salt, eggs, rice, salad, or a sandwich.
personalNotes: This was really good. The steak was rich and buttery, enough that I was full halfway through. The butter baste is optional; the Wagyu-style ribeye has plenty of fat on its own. I dry-brined it for about 5 hours after thawing from frozen in the fridge. Used Central Market olive-fed Texas Wagyu.
title: Reverse-Seared Wagyu Ribeye
totalTime: 45 minutes plus 4 to 8 hours dry-brine time

author: Robert Carr
description: A Nashville hot chicken sandwich with a pickle-buttermilk brined chicken thigh, a rough, crisp fried crust, spicy hot oil, bright pickle slaw, dill pickles, comeback sauce, and a toasted potato or brioche bun.

groceryIngredients.item: plain shredded cabbage and carrot coleslaw mix

ingredients: 1 large boneless skinless chicken thigh, about 6 to 8 ounces
3 tablespoons pickle juice from a jar of dill pickle chips, divided
1 tablespoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, for the brine
3 cups neutral frying oil, such as peanut, canola, or vegetable oil, or enough for 2 inches in a small pot
1 sturdy potato bun or brioche bun
1/4 cup crinkle-cut hamburger dill pickle chips
3/4 cup plain shredded cabbage and carrot coleslaw mix
1/2 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, for the comeback sauce
instructions: Trim any large loose pieces of fat from the chicken thigh.
If the chicken thigh is very thick, lightly pound it to an even 1/2 to 3/4 inch thickness.
In a bowl, whisk together 1/2 cup buttermilk, 2 tablespoons pickle juice from the dill pickle jar, 1 tablespoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, 1 large egg, and 1/2 teaspoon kosher salt.
Add the chicken thigh to the buttermilk brine and toss until fully coated.
Cover and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, or up to 24 hours.

Make the Nashville spice blend by mixing 1 tablespoon cayenne pepper, 1 teaspoon smoked paprika, 1/2 teaspoon chili powder, 1/2 teaspoon garlic powder, 1/2 teaspoon onion powder, 1 1/2 teaspoons light brown sugar, 1/4 teaspoon mustard powder, 1/4 teaspoon black pepper, 1/4 teaspoon kosher salt, and a pinch MSG if using.

Make the seasoned flour coating by whisking together 3/4 cup all-purpose flour, 2 tablespoons cornstarch, 1/4 teaspoon baking powder, 1 tablespoon cayenne pepper, 1/2 teaspoon smoked paprika, 1/2 teaspoon garlic powder, 1/2 teaspoon onion powder, 1/4 teaspoon black pepper, and 1/2 teaspoon kosher salt.
Drizzle 1 tablespoon of the buttermilk brine into the seasoned flour and rub it in with your fingers until small clumps form.

Make the comeback sauce by stirring together 1 tablespoon mayonnaise, 1 teaspoon ketchup, 1/4 teaspoon Dijon mustard, 1/4 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce, 1/2 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, 1/2 teaspoon pickle juice from the dill pickle jar, a pinch garlic powder, and a pinch smoked paprika.
Refrigerate the comeback sauce until the sandwich is ready to assemble.
Set 1 wire rack inside a rimmed baking sheet for the breaded raw chicken, then set a second clean wire rack inside another rimmed baking sheet for the fried chicken.
Remove one piece of chicken from the brine and let the excess drip off.
Press the chicken firmly into the seasoned flour on both sides.
Dip the chicken briefly back into the brine.
Press the chicken into the seasoned flour a second time, packing the flour onto the surface to build a rough crust.
Gently shake off any loose dry flour so the coating looks rough but not dusty.
Place the breaded chicken on the raw-chicken wire rack.
Let the breaded chicken rest for 15 to 20 minutes while the oil heats.
Add 3 cups neutral frying oil to a small Dutch oven or heavy-bottomed pot, or use enough oil for 2 inches of depth, making sure the pot is not filled more than halfway.
Heat the oil to 350°F.

Make the slaw by mixing 3/4 cup plain shredded cabbage and carrot coleslaw mix, 1 tablespoon mayonnaise, 1 1/2 teaspoons apple cider vinegar, 1/2 teaspoon pickle juice from the dill pickle jar, 1/2 teaspoon honey, a pinch celery seed, a pinch black pepper, and a small pinch of kosher salt.
Taste the slaw and adjust with more vinegar, pickle juice, honey, or salt until it tastes bright, tangy, and lightly creamy.
Spread 1/2 tablespoon softened unsalted butter over the cut sides of the potato bun or brioche bun.
Toast the bun in a dry pan until golden brown, then set aside.
Fry the chicken, keeping the oil between 325°F and 350°F, until deeply golden brown and the center reaches 175°F to 185°F, about 6 to 8 minutes depending on thickness.
Transfer the fried chicken to the clean wire rack so steam can escape.
Let the fried chicken rest for 3 to 5 minutes so the juices settle and the crust stays crisp.
Carefully ladle 2 tablespoons of the hot frying oil into the Nashville spice blend.
Whisk until the spices form a glossy red hot oil.
Brush the hot oil generously over both sides of the fried chicken just before assembling the sandwich.
Spread comeback sauce on the toasted bottom bun.
Add 1/4 cup crinkle-cut hamburger dill pickle chips to the bottom bun.
Add the Nashville hot chicken to the sandwich.
Top the sandwich with creamy slaw.
Spread comeback sauce on the toasted top bun.
Close the sandwich and serve immediately.
notes: Use boneless skinless chicken thighs because they stay juicy and are more forgiving than chicken breast.
For the pickle juice, buy a 16-ounce jar of crinkle-cut hamburger dill pickle chips or kosher dill pickle chips and use the liquid from that jar.
Avoid bread-and-butter pickles, sweet pickles, spicy-sweet pickles, and half-sour fermented pickles for this recipe. The pickle juice should taste salty, sour, dill-forward, and lightly garlicky.
Use a thin Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce. It should be vinegar-forward, salty, and peppery, not smoky, fruity, sweet, or extremely thick.
The brine is best after 8 to 12 hours, but 4 hours works and up to 24 hours is fine.
The pickle juice gives the chicken extra salt, acidity, and classic fried chicken sandwich flavor.
MSG is monosodium glutamate. It is a savory seasoning that makes the spice blend taste more savory.
For the juiciest texture, fry thighs past the safe minimum and aim for 175°F to 185°F in the center.
The small clumps in the seasoned flour help create a rougher, crunchier crust.
Resting the breaded chicken before frying helps the coating stick.
Use separate racks for raw breaded chicken and fried chicken. The clean rack also keeps the bottom crust from steaming soft.
The hot oil should be mixed with the spices after frying, not before, so the sugar and cayenne do not burn.
Use 1 tablespoon cayenne in the hot oil for a hot sandwich. Use 2 teaspoons for medium heat or 1 1/2 tablespoons for very hot.
The coleslaw mix should be a plain shredded cabbage and carrot mix, not a salad kit with dressing included.
The slaw should taste tangy and fresh on its own; that acidity is what keeps the sandwich from feeling heavy.
The comeback sauce, slaw, and pickles are important because they balance the heat and richness.
Serve the sandwich right after brushing the chicken with hot oil for the best crust.
Leftover chicken reheats best on a wire rack in a 375°F oven or air fryer until hot and crisp.
title: Nashville Hot Chicken Sandwich
totalTime: 50 minutes plus 4 to 24 hours brine time

author: Robert Carr
description: A double smash burger with cold 80/20 beef, a thin crisp griddle crust, shaved onion smashed into the patties, melted American cheese, a toasted potato bun, dill pickles, crisp lettuce, and homemade burger sauce.
equipment: Cast-iron griddle or large cast-iron skillet
Heavy metal spatula or burger press
ingredients: 6 ounces cold 80/20 ground beef, preferably freshly ground chuck or a chuck and short rib blend
1 small yellow onion, 1 teaspoon very finely grated for sauce and enough of the rest shaved paper-thin for the patties
1 teaspoon beef tallow or neutral oil, optional
1/4 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, such as Frank's RedHot, Crystal, or Texas Pete
1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt or 1/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt, for seasoning the patties
instructions: Grate 1 teaspoon from the yellow onion into a small bowl for the sauce, then shave enough of the remaining onion paper-thin to loosely cover 2 patties.
Spread the shaved onion on a paper towel-lined plate and pat very dry. Wet onion steams the beef instead of browning with it.

Make the burger sauce by stirring together 2 tablespoons mayonnaise, 1 1/2 teaspoons ketchup, 1 teaspoon yellow mustard, 1 teaspoon pickle brine, the 1 teaspoon very finely grated onion and any onion juice, 1/4 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce, 1/4 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, a pinch smoked paprika, and a pinch garlic powder.
Cover and refrigerate the sauce for at least 15 minutes while preparing the burger so the onion, pickle brine, and spices season the mayonnaise.
Mix 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, 1/4 teaspoon black pepper, and a pinch MSG if using in a small bowl. This is the patty seasoning.
Divide 6 ounces cold ground beef into 2 loose 3-ounce portions.
Gently shape each portion into a rough ball without packing the meat tightly.
Place the beef balls on a sheet pan and refrigerate for 15 minutes while preparing the bun and preheating the pan.
Spread the 1/2 tablespoon softened butter evenly over the cut sides of the potato bun.

Preheat a cast-iron griddle or large cast-iron skillet over medium-high to high heat for 5 to 8 minutes, until a drop of water skitters and evaporates quickly or an infrared thermometer reads about 425 degrees F to 475 degrees F.
Toast the buttered bun cut-side down until golden brown, then set it aside.
After the bun is toasted, return the pan to medium-high to high heat for 1 minute so it recovers before the beef goes in.
If the cooking surface is dry, add 1 teaspoon beef tallow or neutral oil and spread it into a thin film with the spatula.
Cook the 2 beef balls together only if the pan is wide enough for both patties to have room.

Place each beef ball on the hot pan, immediately cover with a parchment square, and smash very hard with a burger press or heavy metal spatula for 8 to 10 seconds, until the patty is about 1/4 inch thick with ragged edges.
Remove the parchment and season the top of each patty with half of the salt, pepper, and MSG mixture, using all of the seasoning across the 2 patties.
Scatter half of the shaved onion over the raw top of each smashed patty.
Cook without moving for 90 seconds to 2 minutes, until the edges are browned and crisp.
Use a bench scraper or stiff spatula to scrape firmly under each patty so the crust stays attached to the beef.
Flip each patty onion-side down.
Immediately place 1 slice American cheese on each patty.
Cook for 45 to 75 seconds more, until the onions brown underneath, the cheese melts, and the beef is cooked through to 160 degrees F if checking with an instant-read thermometer.
Stack the 2 patties together for the burger.
Spread 1 1/2 tablespoons burger sauce on the toasted bottom bun.
Add 1/2 cup shredded lettuce and 1/4 cup dill pickle chips to the bottom bun.
Place the double stack of smash patties on the prepared bottom bun.
Spread 1 tablespoon burger sauce on the toasted top bun and serve any remaining sauce on the side.
Close the burger and serve immediately while the edges are still crisp.
notes: The big flavor moves are cold 80/20 beef, a ripping-hot pan, an immediate hard smash, dry shaved onion, American cheese, pickles, and a sauce with pickle brine and grated onion.
Do not mix salt into the ground beef before shaping. Salting the inside can make the patties tighter and more sausage-like.
Smash only once, right after the beef hits the pan. Pressing later squeezes out fat and juices.
A loosely packed 3-ounce ball gives the best ratio of crispy crust to juicy middle for a double burger.
American cheese is intentional here because it melts fast enough to glue the two thin patties together.
Potato buns are ideal because they are soft, slightly sweet, and sturdy enough for sauce and beef fat.

Use a thin Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, such as Frank's RedHot, Crystal, or Texas Pete. Avoid smoky chipotle hot sauce, sweet hot sauce, fermented hot sauce, or extra-hot sauces here because they can overpower the burger sauce.
If the onions look wet after slicing, pat them dry again before cooking.
If the pan starts smoking aggressively or the onions burn before the cheese melts, lower the heat slightly for the next batch.
If the patties gray before they crust, the pan is too cool or too crowded.
Use a sharp-edged metal spatula or bench scraper. A flexible silicone spatula will not scrape the crust cleanly from the pan.
For a classic burger, skip the shaved onions and cook the patties plain. The onion smash version has more diner-style onion flavor.
For USDA guidance, cook ground beef to 160 degrees F. These thin patties cook very quickly, so the main quality risk is overcooking after the crust has already formed.
Serve immediately. Smash burgers lose the magic as the lacy edges steam inside the bun.
title: Double Onion Smash Burger
totalTime: 30 minutes plus 15 minutes beef chill time

additionalTime: 30 minutes to 2 hours buttermilk soak plus 15 minutes coating rest and 3 minutes final rest
author: Robert Carr
description: Chicken fried steak with evenly tenderized top round or cube steak, a seasoned hot sauce buttermilk soak, a rough two-layer flour coating, carefully managed shallow fry, and peppered cream gravy built from clean toasted pan drippings.
equipment: Cast-iron skillet or heavy saute pan
Oven, optional for holding steak warm

groceryIngredients.item: tenderized top round steak or cube steak, ideally 1/3 inch thick and no thicker than 1/2 inch
neutral frying oil, preferably peanut oil

ingredients: 1 tenderized top round steak or cube steak, about 6 to 8 ounces and about 1/3 inch thick, up to 1/2 inch max
1 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, such as Frank's RedHot, Crystal, or Texas Pete
1 1/2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt or 1 teaspoon Morton kosher salt, divided
1 1/4 teaspoons black pepper, freshly ground if possible, divided
Small pinch ground white pepper, for the gravy
Small pinch celery salt, for the gravy
2 cups neutral frying oil, preferably peanut oil, or enough for 1/2 inch depth in a small skillet
1 tablespoon finely chopped fresh parsley, optional, for finishing
instructions: Set 1 wire rack inside a rimmed baking sheet for the breaded raw steak, then set a second clean wire rack inside another rimmed baking sheet for the fried steak.
Pat the tenderized top round steak or cube steak very dry with paper towels.

If starting with plain top round instead of already-tenderized cube steak, cover the steak with plastic wrap or parchment and pound it with the textured side of a meat mallet until it is evenly tenderized and about 1/3 inch thick, but no thicker than 1/2 inch.
If an already-tenderized steak is thicker than 1/2 inch, cover it with plastic wrap or parchment and gently pound it to an even 1/3 to 1/2 inch thickness with a meat mallet or rolling pin.
Season both sides of the steak with 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt and 1/8 teaspoon black pepper.
Let the seasoned steak sit for 10 minutes while you make the buttermilk mixture so the salt starts dissolving into the surface instead of staying dry on top.
In a shallow bowl, whisk together 1/2 cup buttermilk, 1 large egg, 1 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, and 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
Add the seasoned steak to the buttermilk mixture and turn until fully coated.
Cover and refrigerate the steak in the buttermilk mixture for at least 30 minutes, or up to 2 hours for deeper seasoning without making the coating or meat taste overly tangy.
About 20 minutes before breading, remove the bowl of steak from the refrigerator so the steak is not ice-cold when it hits the oil.
Before breading, measure 2 tablespoons all-purpose flour into a clean small bowl and reserve it for the gravy so it never touches raw steak.

Make the seasoned flour coating in a shallow bowl by whisking together the remaining 1/2 cup plus 2 tablespoons all-purpose flour, 2 tablespoons cornstarch, 1/4 teaspoon baking powder, 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, 1/2 teaspoon black pepper, 1/2 teaspoon smoked paprika, 1/4 teaspoon garlic powder, 1/4 teaspoon onion powder, 1/8 teaspoon cayenne pepper, 1/8 teaspoon mustard powder, and 1/8 teaspoon MSG if using.

Drizzle 1 tablespoon of the buttermilk mixture into the seasoned flour and rub it through the flour with your fingers until the flour has pea-size clumps. Those clumps become the extra-crunchy ridges.
Remove 1 steak from the buttermilk mixture and let the excess drip back into the bowl for 5 seconds.
Press the steak firmly into the seasoned flour on both sides, making sure flour gets into every tenderized groove.
Dip the floured steak back into the buttermilk mixture for 1 to 2 seconds, then let the excess drip off again.
Press the steak into the seasoned flour a second time, packing the clumpy flour onto both sides until there are no damp bare spots.
Shake off only loose dusty flour, then place the breaded steak on the raw-steak wire rack.
Let the breaded steak rest on the raw-steak wire rack for 15 minutes so the flour coating absorbs moisture and sticks during frying.
While the coating rests, add 2 cups neutral frying oil to a small cast-iron skillet or heavy saute pan, or use enough oil to reach 1/2 inch depth.
Heat the oil over medium-high heat to 350 degrees F.
Fry the steak without crowding. If using a very small skillet, make sure the steak can lie flat in the oil.
Keep the oil between 325 degrees F and 350 degrees F while frying, adjusting the burner as needed after each steak goes in.
Cook each steak for 3 to 4 minutes on the first side, until the bottom is deep golden brown and crisp.
Flip with tongs and cook the second side for 2 to 4 minutes more, until deep golden brown and the center reaches at least 145 degrees F when checked through the side of the steak.
Use a fine-mesh skimmer or slotted spoon to remove loose fried crumbs if they start turning dark brown or black so the gravy does not taste bitter.
Transfer the fried steak to the clean wire rack and immediately sprinkle it with a tiny pinch of Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
Let the fried steak rest on the clean wire rack for at least 3 minutes before serving.
Carefully pour the hot oil into a heat-safe bowl or measuring cup, leaving the browned bits in the skillet.
Inspect the skillet before making gravy. Keep golden brown bits and toasted crumbs, but if any blackened flour crumbs smell burnt or harsh, carefully wipe those out with paper towels held in tongs.
Return 1 tablespoon of the frying oil to the skillet with the good browned bits and place the skillet over medium heat.
Add 1 tablespoon unsalted butter to the frying oil and let it melt.
Whisk the reserved 2 tablespoons clean all-purpose flour into the oil and butter until no dry flour remains.
Cook the flour and fat mixture for 2 to 3 minutes, whisking constantly, until it smells toasted and turns light tan.
Slowly whisk in 3/4 cup whole milk, adding a splash at a time and whisking smooth after each addition.

Whisk in 2 tablespoons heavy cream, 2 tablespoons low-sodium beef broth, 1/4 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce, the remaining 1/4 teaspoon garlic powder, the remaining 1/2 teaspoon black pepper, a small pinch ground white pepper, a small pinch celery salt, and 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
Simmer the gravy for 4 to 6 minutes, whisking often and scraping the browned bits from the bottom of the skillet, until thick enough to coat a spoon but still loose enough to pour.
If the gravy becomes thicker than you like, whisk in the remaining 1/4 cup whole milk a few tablespoons at a time until it is creamy and pourable.
Taste the gravy and adjust only if needed with a small pinch of kosher salt, black pepper, Worcestershire sauce, or hot sauce.
Spoon hot pepper gravy over the chicken fried steak right before serving so the crust stays crisp as long as possible.
Finish with 1 tablespoon finely chopped fresh parsley if using.
notes: Best meat choice: ask the butcher for top round cutlets tenderized once or twice, ideally about 1/3 inch thick and no thicker than 1/2 inch. That gives enough steak texture without making the crust over-brown before the meat is done.
Packaged cube steak is still the right classic option if it looks fresh, evenly tenderized, and about 1/3 to 1/2 inch thick. Avoid grey, wet-looking, ragged, paper-thin pieces.
Do not use cubed stew meat. Cube steak should look like thin steak with small tenderized marks across the surface, not chunks of beef.
If using Morton kosher salt instead of Diamond Crystal, use about two-thirds of the salt amounts listed in the instructions because Morton is denser by volume.
Do not extend the buttermilk soak past about 2 hours. The steak is already tenderized, and a very long acidic soak can push the texture in a softer, less steak-like direction.
Do not skip reserving clean flour for the gravy. Flour used to coat raw steak has touched raw beef and should not be used for quick gravy unless it is cooked thoroughly for food safety.

The biggest flavor moves are seasoning the meat before soaking, using hot sauce in the buttermilk, making clumps in the flour coating, coating the steak twice, frying on a rack instead of paper towels, and building the gravy from clean seasoned pan drippings.
The 15 minute coating rest matters because dry flour patches absorb moisture and the crust is less likely to slide off in the oil.
Peanut oil gives the cleanest classic fried flavor, but canola or vegetable oil still works well.
The oil temperature will drop when steak goes in. Start at 350 degrees F, then manage the burner so the oil stays mostly between 325 degrees F and 350 degrees F.
Skim loose crumbs if they start getting too dark. Burnt flour specks can make the gravy taste bitter.
A clean wire rack keeps the bottom crust crisp after frying. Paper towels trap steam and can soften the breading.
If you make a second steak, a 200 degrees F oven hold keeps the first steak hot without steaming the crust. Keep it uncovered on the rack and avoid holding longer than about 20 minutes.

Tenderized round steak and cube steak are lean and relatively thin, so the quality risk is overcooking after the crust is already brown. Pull the steak once the coating is golden brown and the center is at least 145 degrees F, then rest for at least 3 minutes.
Use the browned bits in the skillet for gravy only if they smell toasted and savory. If they smell burnt, remove the blackened crumbs and keep only the clean browned bits.
The beef broth, Worcestershire sauce, white pepper, and celery salt are small but important. They make the cream gravy taste savory instead of flat.
For a thicker diner-style gravy, simmer the gravy 1 to 2 minutes longer before adding any extra milk. For a looser gravy, use the reserved 1/2 cup whole milk.
Serve immediately with mashed potatoes, buttered corn, green beans, or a simple salad.
title: Chicken Fried Steak
totalTime: 1 hour plus 30 minutes to 2 hours buttermilk soak

additionalTime: 8 to 24 hours pork marinade plus 1 hour 30 minutes dough rise
author: Robert Carr
description: Mojo roast pork Cubano sandwiches with homemade Cuban-style rolls, citrus-garlic pork shoulder, ham, Swiss cheese, quick garlic pickles, mustard sauce, and a buttered pressed crust.
equipment: Cast-iron skillet, griddle, or panini press
Dutch oven or roasting pan with lid
Heavy skillet or foil-wrapped brick, if not using a panini press
Large bowl or stand mixer with dough hook
ingredients: 5/8 cup warm water, 105 to 110 degrees F, for the rolls
1/4 cup water, for the quick pickles
1 1/2 tablespoons melted lard or melted unsalted butter, for the rolls
3 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt or 2 teaspoons Morton kosher salt, divided
1 1/2 pounds boneless pork shoulder, trimmed of thick exterior fat and cut into 2 large pieces
2 large oranges, zested and juiced to yield 1 1/2 teaspoons zest and 6 tablespoons juice
2 limes, juiced to yield 1/4 cup juice
1/2 lemon, juiced to yield 1 tablespoon juice
1 tablespoon finely chopped fresh oregano leaves and tender stems, or 1 teaspoon dried oregano
1/2 large English cucumber, sliced 1/8 inch thick, or 6 ounces small pickling cucumbers
2 tablespoons fresh dill sprigs or chopped dill
4 ounces Swiss cheese, preferably 4 thin slices
1 1/2 tablespoons unsalted butter, softened, for pressing the sandwiches
instructions: Pat the 1 1/2 pounds boneless pork shoulder dry with paper towels.
Trim any thick exterior fat down to about 1/4 inch, then cut the pork shoulder into 2 large pieces so the mojo can season more surface area.
Season the pork all over with 1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt and 1/2 teaspoon black pepper.

In a blender or food processor, combine 1 1/2 teaspoons orange zest, 6 tablespoons fresh orange juice, 1/4 cup fresh lime juice, 1 tablespoon fresh lemon juice, 2 tablespoons olive oil, 5 garlic cloves, 1 tablespoon chopped fresh oregano, 1 teaspoon ground cumin, 1/2 teaspoon smoked paprika, 1 1/2 teaspoons Worcestershire sauce, and 1/2 teaspoon black pepper.
Blend the mojo for 20 to 30 seconds, until the garlic is finely chopped and the marinade looks cloudy and blended.
Place the sliced yellow onion and 1 dried bay leaf in a nonreactive bowl, zip-top bag, or covered container.
Add the seasoned pork pieces to the onion and bay leaves, then pour all of the mojo over the pork.
Turn the pork pieces until every side is coated in mojo, then cover and refrigerate for 8 to 24 hours.
Remove the marinated pork from the refrigerator 30 minutes before roasting.
Heat the oven to 300 degrees F.
Transfer the pork, all of the mojo, the sliced onion, and the bay leaves to a Dutch oven or covered roasting pan.
Add 1/4 cup low-sodium chicken broth around the pork, cover tightly, and roast for 1 hour 45 minutes.
After 1 hour 45 minutes, uncover the pot and spoon the pan juices over the pork.

Continue roasting uncovered for 35 to 55 minutes, spooning the pan juices over the pork every 15 minutes, until the pork is browned at the edges, tender enough to slice or pull, and the thickest piece reaches 190 to 200 degrees F.

While the pork starts roasting, make the roll dough by stirring together 5/8 cup warm water, 1 tablespoon granulated sugar, and 1 1/8 teaspoons active dry yeast in a large bowl or the bowl of a stand mixer.
Let the yeast mixture stand for 5 to 10 minutes, until it looks foamy on top.
Add 2 cups bread flour, 1 1/2 tablespoons melted lard or melted unsalted butter, and 1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt to the yeast mixture.

Mix on low speed with a dough hook until no dry flour remains, then knead on medium-low for 8 minutes. If mixing by hand, stir with a sturdy spoon until shaggy, then knead on a lightly floured counter for about 10 minutes.
The dough should be smooth and slightly tacky but should not paste itself to your fingers; if it is very sticky, knead for 1 more minute before adding any extra flour.
Cover the dough bowl and let the dough rise at warm room temperature for 60 to 90 minutes, until doubled.
Line a rimmed baking sheet with parchment paper.
Turn the risen dough onto a lightly floured counter, divide it into 2 equal pieces, and shape each piece into a tight 7-inch sandwich roll.
Place the 2 shaped rolls on the parchment-lined baking sheet, leaving at least 2 inches between them.
Cover the shaped rolls with a clean towel and let them rise for 35 to 45 minutes, until puffy.
If the rolls finish the second rise before the pork is tender, keep them covered at room temperature for up to 30 minutes.
When the pork is tender, transfer it to a cutting board, tent it loosely with foil, and let it rest for at least 20 minutes while the rolls bake.
Increase the oven to 400 degrees F for the rolls.
Use a sharp knife to make one shallow lengthwise slash down the top of each roll.
Bake the 2 rolls for 16 to 20 minutes, until golden brown and about 195 degrees F in the center.
Move the rolls to a wire rack and cool for at least 20 minutes before slicing.
Make the quick pickles by placing the sliced English cucumber and 2 tablespoons fresh dill in a heat-safe bowl or jar.

In a small saucepan, combine 1/4 cup white vinegar, 1/4 cup water, 1 1/2 teaspoons granulated sugar, 3/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, 1/2 teaspoon yellow mustard seed, and the remaining 1 smashed garlic clove.
Bring the pickle brine to a simmer, stirring until the sugar and salt dissolve.
Pour the hot pickle brine over the cucumber and dill, press the cucumber under the liquid, and let the pickles cool for at least 30 minutes.
Strain the pan juices through a fine mesh strainer, skim off excess surface fat with a spoon, and reserve at least 1/4 cup strained pan juices for brushing the pork and seasoning the sauce.

Make the mustard sauce by stirring together 1/4 cup yellow mustard, 1 tablespoon Dijon mustard, 2 tablespoons mayonnaise, 1 1/2 teaspoons honey, 1 1/2 teaspoons cooled quick pickle brine, 1 1/2 teaspoons strained pork pan juices, 1/8 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/8 teaspoon black pepper.
Slice or pull the rested pork into bite-size pieces, then toss the pork with 1 tablespoon strained pan juices so it stays juicy but not wet.
Split the 2 cooled rolls lengthwise without cutting all the way through if possible.
Spread about 1 tablespoon mustard sauce on each cut side of each roll, using about 2 tablespoons sauce per sandwich.
Layer 3 ounces thin-sliced smoked deli ham onto each bottom roll.
Add about 5 ounces mojo roast pork to each sandwich, using about half of the sliced or pulled pork.
Add 2 ounces Swiss cheese to each sandwich, preferably 2 thin slices per sandwich.
Drain the quick pickles well, then add about 1/4 cup pickles to each sandwich.
Close the sandwiches and spread the outside of the rolls with 1 1/2 tablespoons softened unsalted butter total, coating the tops and bottoms.
Heat a cast-iron skillet, griddle, or panini press over medium heat.
Press 1 or 2 sandwiches at a time for 3 to 5 minutes per side, weighing them down with a heavy skillet or foil-wrapped brick if using a skillet or griddle.
Cook until the bread is well toasted, the Swiss cheese is melted, and the center of each sandwich is hot.
Let the pressed sandwiches rest for 2 minutes, then cut each sandwich in half and serve immediately.
notes: The main flavor comes from citrus-garlic mojo in the pork, pork drippings in the mustard sauce, quick pickles, ham, melted Swiss cheese, and a buttered pressed roll.
For boneless pork shoulder, buy a piece with good marbling and a small fat cap. If using bone-in pork shoulder, buy about 4 pounds so you still have enough meat after removing the bone.
The pork is best after 8 to 12 hours in the mojo. Up to 24 hours is fine, but avoid going longer because the citrus can make the outside of the pork seem cured.
If using Morton kosher salt instead of Diamond Crystal, use about two-thirds of the salt amounts listed in the instructions because Morton is denser by volume.
Fresh oregano gives the mojo a greener, brighter flavor. If using dried oregano, use 2 teaspoons dried oregano instead of 2 tablespoons fresh oregano.
The pork should be tender enough to slice or pull but not swimming in liquid. Toss it with only 2 tablespoons pan juices before building the sandwiches so the bread can still crisp.
The rolls can be baked 1 day ahead. Rewarm them in a 300 degrees F oven for 5 to 7 minutes before assembling.
Drain the quick pickles well before adding them to the sandwiches. Wet pickles can keep the bread from crisping in the press.
Medium heat is better than high heat for pressing because the bread needs time to toast while the cheese melts and the middle gets hot.
A panini press works well. If using a skillet or griddle, place a sheet of parchment over the sandwich before weighing it down with a heavy skillet or foil-wrapped brick.
Leftover mojo pork is good in rice bowls, tacos, breakfast hash, or more sandwiches.
title: Mojo Roast Pork Cubano Sandwiches
totalTime: 4 hours plus 8 to 24 hours pork marinade

additionalTime: 10 minutes quick soak plus 10 minutes coating rest
author: Robert Carr
description: A quick hot honey chicken cutlet sandwich with a thin chicken breast cutlet, a buttermilk-hot sauce dip, crisp panko crust, garlic mayo, lime slaw, dill pickles, and a toasted potato bun.
equipment: Cast-iron skillet or heavy saute pan

groceryIngredients.item: plain shredded cabbage and carrot coleslaw mix
groceryIngredients.display: plain shredded cabbage and carrot coleslaw mix

ingredients: 1 boneless skinless chicken breast cutlet, about 6 ounces
1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt or 1/2 teaspoon Morton kosher salt, divided
1/2 teaspoon black pepper, freshly ground if possible, divided
1 tablespoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, such as Frank's RedHot, Crystal, or Texas Pete, divided
1 (16-ounce) jar dill pickle chips, using 1/4 cup pickle chips and 2 tablespoons pickle brine
3/4 cup avocado oil or another neutral high-heat oil, or enough for a 1/4-inch layer
3/4 cup plain shredded cabbage and carrot coleslaw mix
instructions: Set 1 wire rack inside a rimmed baking sheet for the breaded raw chicken, then set a second clean wire rack inside another rimmed baking sheet for the fried chicken.
If the chicken breast is not already a thin cutlet, split it horizontally and save the extra cutlet for another meal.
Place the chicken cutlet between sheets of plastic wrap or parchment and pound it to an even 1/2 inch thickness.
Season both sides of the chicken cutlet with 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt and 1/8 teaspoon black pepper.
In a shallow bowl, whisk together 1/4 cup buttermilk, 1 large egg, 1 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, 1 teaspoon Dijon mustard, and 1 teaspoon pickle brine from the dill pickle jar.
Add the seasoned chicken cutlet to the buttermilk mixture and turn until fully coated.
Let the chicken sit in the buttermilk mixture at room temperature for 10 minutes while making the sauce and slaw.

Make the garlic mayo by stirring together 2 tablespoons mayonnaise, 1 teaspoon pickle brine, 1/2 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, 1 teaspoon Dijon mustard, 1/2 teaspoon honey, a pinch smoked paprika, a pinch garlic powder, and a tiny pinch Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
Refrigerate the garlic mayo until assembly.

Make the lime slaw by whisking together 1 tablespoon mayonnaise, 1 teaspoon fresh lime juice, 1 teaspoon apple cider vinegar, 1 teaspoon pickle brine, 1/2 teaspoon honey, a tiny pinch Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and a pinch black pepper in a medium bowl.
Add 3/4 cup plain shredded cabbage and carrot coleslaw mix and the thinly sliced green onion to the lime dressing.
Toss the slaw for 30 seconds, until all of the cabbage is lightly coated, then refrigerate it until assembly.

Make the dry coating in a wide shallow bowl by whisking together 1/4 cup all-purpose flour, 1/3 cup panko breadcrumbs, 1 tablespoon cornstarch, 1/4 teaspoon baking powder, 1/4 teaspoon smoked paprika, 1/4 teaspoon garlic powder, 1/4 teaspoon onion powder, 1/8 teaspoon cayenne pepper, 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, 1/4 teaspoon black pepper, and a pinch MSG if using.
Drizzle 1 tablespoon of the buttermilk mixture into the dry coating and rub it through with your fingers until small shaggy clumps form.
Remove 1 chicken cutlet from the buttermilk mixture and let the excess drip back into the bowl for 3 to 5 seconds.
Press the chicken cutlet firmly into the dry coating on both sides.
Dip the coated chicken cutlet back into the buttermilk mixture for 1 second, then let the excess drip off.
Press the chicken cutlet into the dry coating a second time, packing the panko-flour mixture onto both sides until there are no wet spots.
Place the breaded chicken cutlet on the raw-chicken wire rack.
Let the breaded chicken rest on the raw-chicken wire rack for 10 minutes so the coating absorbs moisture and sticks.
While the chicken rests, spread 1/2 tablespoon softened unsalted butter over the cut sides of the potato bun.
Heat the cast-iron skillet or heavy saute pan over medium heat, then toast the buttered bun cut-side down for 1 to 2 minutes, until golden brown.
Move the toasted bun to a plate and wipe out any loose crumbs from the skillet.
Pour 3/4 cup avocado oil into the skillet, or enough to make a 1/4-inch layer.
Heat the oil over medium to medium-high heat until it reaches 350 degrees F.
Fry the chicken cutlet without crowding the pan.
Cook the first side for 3 to 4 minutes, until deep golden brown and crisp.
Flip the chicken cutlet with tongs and cook the second side for 2 to 3 minutes, until deep golden brown and the center reaches 165 degrees F.
Transfer the fried chicken cutlet to the clean wire rack so steam can escape.

Make the hot honey butter by warming the remaining 1/2 tablespoon unsalted butter, 1 tablespoon honey, 1 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, 1/2 teaspoon apple cider vinegar, the remaining 1/8 teaspoon cayenne pepper, and a tiny pinch Diamond Crystal kosher salt in a small saucepan or microwave-safe bowl until the butter melts.
Stir the hot honey butter until glossy and smooth.
Brush or spoon the hot honey butter over both sides of the fried chicken cutlet while it is still hot.
Spread about 1 tablespoon garlic mayo on the toasted bottom bun.
Add 1/4 cup dill pickle chips to the bottom bun.
Place the hot honey chicken cutlet on the bottom bun.
Top the chicken cutlet with about 1/2 cup lime slaw.
Spread about 1 tablespoon garlic mayo on the toasted top bun.
Close the sandwich and serve immediately while the chicken crust is crisp.
notes: Use a thin Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce. It should taste salty, vinegary, and peppery, not smoky or sweet.
Buy dill pickle chips, not sweet bread-and-butter pickles, because the brine seasons the chicken dip, garlic mayo, and slaw.
Pounding the chicken to 1/2 inch matters because the coating browns quickly; thick chicken can overcook on the outside before the center reaches 165 degrees F.
The 10-minute coating rest helps the dry flour patches hydrate so the crust clings in the skillet.
Use separate racks for raw breaded chicken and fried chicken. The clean rack also keeps the bottom crust from steaming soft.
Keep the oil between 325 degrees F and 350 degrees F while frying. If the coating browns before the chicken reaches 165 degrees F, lower the heat slightly.
The slaw should stay lightly dressed, not wet. If the coleslaw mix releases liquid while waiting, lift the slaw with tongs and leave extra dressing behind during assembly.
For a less spicy sandwich, skip the cayenne pepper in the dry coating and hot honey butter.
For a hotter sandwich, add 1/8 to 1/4 teaspoon extra cayenne pepper to the hot honey butter.
Serve right after brushing with hot honey butter so the crust stays crisp and the bun stays toasted.
title: Hot Honey Chicken Cutlet Sandwich

author: Robert Carr
description: A skillet steak sandwich with thin-sliced seared steak, pepperoncini onions, garlic mayo, melted deli provolone, dressed arugula, and a toasted ciabatta or hoagie roll.
equipment: Cast-iron skillet or heavy saute pan

groceryIngredients.item: top sirloin steak, strip steak, or flat iron steak
ciabatta roll or sturdy hoagie roll

ingredients: 6 to 8 ounces top sirloin steak, strip steak, or flat iron steak, ideally 1 to 1 1/4 inches thick
3/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt or 1/2 teaspoon Morton kosher salt, divided
1/2 teaspoon black pepper, freshly ground if possible, divided
1 teaspoon avocado oil or another neutral high-heat oil
1 (12-ounce) jar sliced pepperoncini peppers, using 2 tablespoons peppers and 1 teaspoon brine
1 small garlic clove, half finely grated and half smashed
1 ciabatta roll or sturdy hoagie roll, split
1/2 ounce baby arugula, about 1/2 packed cup
instructions: Pat the steak very dry with paper towels.
Season both sides of the steak with 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, 1/4 teaspoon black pepper, and 1/4 teaspoon smoked paprika.
Let the seasoned steak sit at room temperature for 15 to 20 minutes while preparing the sauce and onions.

Make the garlic mayo by stirring together 2 tablespoons mayonnaise, 1 teaspoon Dijon mustard, 1/4 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce, 1/2 teaspoon pepperoncini brine, the finely grated garlic, and a pinch of black pepper in a small bowl.
Taste the garlic mayo and add only a few drops more pepperoncini brine if it needs more tang; keep it thick enough to cling to the roll.
Melt 1/2 tablespoon unsalted butter in a cast-iron skillet or heavy saute pan over medium heat.
Add the thinly sliced yellow onion, a small pinch Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and a small pinch black pepper.
Cook the onion for 7 to 9 minutes, stirring every minute, until softened, sweet, and lightly browned at the edges.
Stir 2 tablespoons sliced pepperoncini peppers and the remaining 1/2 teaspoon pepperoncini brine into the onions.
Cook the pepperoncini onions for 1 to 2 minutes, until the brine has mostly evaporated and the onions taste bright and savory without being wet.
Transfer the pepperoncini onions to a plate.
Melt the remaining 1/2 tablespoon unsalted butter in the same skillet over medium heat.
Add the smashed garlic to the melted butter and swirl it for 20 to 30 seconds until fragrant.
Place the split ciabatta roll or hoagie roll cut-side down in the garlic butter and toast for 1 to 2 minutes, until the cut sides are golden.
Move the toasted roll to a plate and discard the smashed garlic.
Wipe out any loose crumbs and browned garlic bits from the skillet.
Increase the heat to medium-high, let the skillet heat for 1 minute, and add 1 teaspoon avocado oil.
Pat the steak dry again if moisture has appeared on the surface.
When the oil is shimmering, add the steak to the skillet.
Sear the steak for 3 to 4 minutes on the first side, without moving it, until well browned.
Flip the steak with tongs and sear the second side for 3 to 4 minutes, until the center reaches 120 to 125 degrees F for medium-rare after the final cheese melt or 130 degrees F for medium.
If the steak is browning faster than it is cooking through, reduce the heat to medium and keep cooking until it reaches the target temperature.
Transfer the steak to a cutting board and let it rest for 5 to 8 minutes.
Slice the rested steak as thinly as possible against the grain, angling the knife slightly for wider slices.
Toss the sliced steak with any cutting board juices and a tiny pinch of Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
Return the sliced steak and any board juices to the skillet over low heat and gather it into 1 loose pile.
Top the steak pile with the pepperoncini onions and 1 slice deli provolone cheese.
Cover the skillet for 1 to 2 minutes, just until the provolone melts over the steak and onions.
While the cheese melts, toss 1/2 ounce baby arugula with 1/2 teaspoon fresh lemon juice, 1/4 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil, and a tiny pinch of Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
Spread about 1 tablespoon garlic mayo on the toasted bottom roll.
Use tongs or a spatula to move the cheesy steak-and-onion pile onto the bottom roll.
Top the sandwich with the dressed arugula.
Spread about 1 tablespoon garlic mayo on the toasted top roll.
Close the sandwich and serve immediately while the roll is crisp and the cheese is melted.
notes: For speed, season the steak first, make the garlic mayo and onions while the salt starts working, then sear, slice, melt, and assemble.
For even better seasoning, salt the steak up to 24 hours ahead and refrigerate it uncovered on a plate or rack. Bring it out while preparing the sauce and onions.
Top sirloin works well here because it cooks quickly and slices well. Strip steak is richer; flat iron is very tender if sliced across the grain.
Avoid very thick steaks for this quick version. A 1 to 1 1/4 inch steak gives enough browning time without turning the recipe into a steakhouse project.
If using two smaller steak pieces, cook them one at a time. Crowding the skillet makes the steak steam instead of sear.
Pull the steak a few degrees below the final doneness you want because the sliced meat warms again while the cheese melts.
Use deli provolone for better melt and flavor. Provolone piccante is excellent here if you like a sharper cheese.
Use sliced pepperoncini peppers from a jar, not sweet banana peppers, because the brine adds acidity that cuts through the steak, mayo, butter, and cheese.
The garlic mayo should taste tangy, savory, and lightly sharp. If it tastes flat, add a few drops of pepperoncini brine before adding more salt.
Resting the steak for 5 to 8 minutes keeps the juices in the meat instead of soaking the bread.
Slice against the grain as thinly as possible. Thin slices make a quick steak sandwich feel tender without needing a marinade.
Dress the arugula right before assembly so it stays peppery and fresh instead of wilting.
Serve immediately. The best texture is crisp toasted bread, warm steak, melted cheese, bright onions, and fresh arugula all at once.
title: Garlic Steak Sandwich

additionalTime: 30 minutes to 12 hours marinating plus 20 minutes beef chill time
author: Robert Carr
description: Korean BBQ bulgogi with thin-sliced beef, Asian pear, soy sauce, garlic, ginger, sesame, lightly charred vegetables, and a reserved glaze for finishing in the skillet.
equipment: Large cast-iron skillet, carbon-steel skillet, or flat-top griddle

groceryIngredients.item: boneless ribeye, top sirloin, or skirt steak
butter lettuce or red leaf lettuce

ingredients: 1 pound boneless ribeye, top sirloin, or skirt steak, chilled until firm and sliced across the grain 1/8 inch thick, or Korean-market pre-sliced bulgogi beef
1/2 medium Asian pear or Bosc pear, peeled and cored, using 1/4 cup finely grated pear and juice
1 small yellow onion, half finely grated for 2 tablespoons onion and juice and half thinly sliced for cooking
3 garlic cloves, finely grated or minced
1 1/2 teaspoons peeled fresh ginger, finely grated
3 green onions, 2 thinly sliced for the marinade and 1 thinly sliced for garnish
4 ounces fresh shiitake or cremini mushrooms, stems trimmed and caps sliced 1/4 inch thick
1/2 medium carrot, peeled and cut into thin matchsticks
1/2 teaspoon black pepper, freshly ground if possible
Small pinch Diamond Crystal kosher salt or Morton kosher salt, for the vegetables
1 tablespoon neutral high-heat oil, such as avocado, grapeseed, canola, or vegetable oil, divided
3/4 cup uncooked short-grain white rice, cooked for serving
1 head butter lettuce or red leaf lettuce, leaves separated, optional for wraps
instructions: If the 1 pound beef is not already thin-sliced, place it on a plate or small sheet pan and freeze it for 20 minutes, just until the outside is firm but not frozen solid.

Slice the chilled 1 pound beef across the grain into pieces about 1/8 inch thick. If any slices are wider than 2 inches, cut them into shorter bite-size strips so they cook quickly and are easy to pick up with rice or lettuce.
Finely grate enough peeled pear to measure 1/4 cup packed pear and juice.
Cut the yellow onion in half, finely grate one half until you have about 2 tablespoons onion and juice, and thinly slice the remaining half for cooking.

In a mixing bowl, whisk together the 1/4 cup grated pear and juice, 2 tablespoons grated onion and juice, 1/4 cup low-sodium soy sauce, 1 1/2 tablespoons packed light brown sugar, 1 tablespoon honey, 1 tablespoon mirin or rice wine, 1 1/2 teaspoons rice vinegar, 1 teaspoon gochujang, 1 1/2 teaspoons toasted sesame oil, 3 finely grated garlic cloves, 1 1/2 teaspoons finely grated ginger, 1/2 teaspoon black pepper, 2 thinly sliced green onions, and 1 1/2 teaspoons toasted sesame seeds.

Before the raw beef goes into the bowl, spoon 2 tablespoons of the clean marinade into a small bowl and stir in the remaining 1/2 teaspoon toasted sesame oil. Set this reserved glaze aside for finishing the cooked bulgogi.
Add the sliced beef to the large bowl with the remaining marinade.
Use clean hands or tongs to separate the beef slices and massage the marinade through the meat for 1 to 2 minutes so every slice is coated.
Cover the bowl and refrigerate the beef for at least 30 minutes and up to 12 hours. For more seasoning without the pear making the meat too soft, aim for 2 to 6 hours.

While the beef marinates, trim and slice the 4 ounces mushrooms, cut the 1/2 medium carrot into thin matchsticks, thinly slice the remaining half onion, thinly slice the remaining green onion for garnish, and cook the 3/4 cup short-grain white rice if serving with rice.
Take the marinated beef out of the refrigerator 15 minutes before cooking so it loses its chill but stays cool.
Set a large plate or sheet pan near the stove for cooked batches.
Heat a large cast-iron skillet, carbon-steel skillet, or flat-top griddle over medium-high to high heat for 4 to 5 minutes, until a drop of water sizzles hard and evaporates quickly.
Add 1 tablespoon neutral high-heat oil to the hot pan.
Add the sliced half onion, 4 ounces sliced mushrooms, 1/2 carrot cut into matchsticks, and a small pinch Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
Cook the vegetables for 3 to 4 minutes, stirring every 30 to 45 seconds, until the onion softens, the mushrooms brown at the edges, and the carrot is crisp-tender.
Transfer the vegetables to the large plate or sheet pan.
Add 1 teaspoon neutral high-heat oil to the pan and let it shimmer for 15 to 20 seconds.
Lift about half of the beef from the marinade with tongs, let the excess marinade drip back into the bowl for a few seconds, and spread the beef into a single layer in the hot pan.
Cook the first batch for 60 to 90 seconds without stirring, until the underside is browned in spots.
Flip and stir the beef for 60 to 90 seconds more, until there are no raw red spots and the edges are caramelized.
Drizzle 1 tablespoon of the reserved clean glaze over the cooked batch, toss for 10 to 15 seconds to coat, then transfer the beef to the plate with the vegetables.
Repeat with the second batch of beef, adding 1 teaspoon neutral high-heat oil before the batch and using the remaining 1 tablespoon reserved clean glaze.
Discard the raw marinade left in the large bowl after the beef is removed.
Return all cooked beef and vegetables to the hot pan.
Add any remaining reserved clean glaze and toss everything together for 30 to 45 seconds, just until the bulgogi is hot and lightly sticky.
Turn off the heat and sprinkle the bulgogi with the remaining 1 1/2 teaspoons toasted sesame seeds and the remaining thinly sliced green onion.
Serve immediately over the cooked short-grain rice, with separated lettuce leaves and kimchi on the side if using.
notes: For easier prep, buy Korean-market pre-sliced bulgogi beef or ask the butcher to slice ribeye, top sirloin, or skirt steak across the grain about 1/8 inch thick.
Ribeye gives the richest, most tender result. Top sirloin is leaner and still works well if sliced thin. Skirt steak has a stronger beef flavor but must be sliced across the grain.
Asian pear adds sweetness and helps tenderize the beef. Bosc pear works as a substitute. Avoid using a large amount of kiwi because it can make thin beef mushy.
Use low-sodium soy sauce. If using regular soy sauce, reduce the soy sauce to 1/3 cup and add 2 tablespoons water to keep the bulgogi from turning too salty.
The reserved glaze must be spooned out before raw beef touches the marinade. Do not use raw leftover marinade as a finishing sauce.
Crowding is the main reason bulgogi turns watery. Cook in 2 batches so the beef browns and caramelizes instead of steaming.
A very hot cast-iron skillet, carbon-steel skillet, grill pan, or flat-top griddle helps the beef brown indoors.
The vegetables are cooked first so they brown instead of steaming under wet beef. Keep them crisp-tender because they will warm again at the end.
Serve with short-grain rice for a bowl, or spoon the bulgogi into lettuce leaves with rice and kimchi for wraps.
Leftovers keep for 3 days in the refrigerator. Reheat quickly in a hot skillet with a splash of water, just until warmed through.
title: Korean BBQ Bulgogi
totalTime: 50 minutes plus 30 minutes to 12 hours marinating

additionalTime: 30 minutes short-rib soak plus 6 to 24 hours marinating
author: Robert Carr
description: Korean BBQ galbi with flanken-cut beef short ribs, pear-soy marinade, garlic, ginger, sesame, scallions, grilled onion, and a simmered glaze for charred edges.
equipment: Outdoor grill, cast-iron skillet, carbon-steel skillet, or grill pan
ingredients: 1 1/2 pounds flanken-cut beef short ribs, cross-cut through the bone 1/4 to 1/2 inch thick, usually 4 to 6 strips
1/2 medium Asian pear or Bosc pear, peeled and cored, using 1/3 cup finely grated pear and juice
1 small yellow onion, half finely grated for 3 tablespoons onion and juice and half sliced into 1/2-inch rings for grilling
4 garlic cloves, finely grated or minced
1 1/2 teaspoons peeled fresh ginger, finely grated
3 green onions, 2 thinly sliced for the marinade and 1 thinly sliced for garnish
3/4 teaspoon black pepper, freshly ground if possible
Small pinch Diamond Crystal kosher salt or Morton kosher salt, for the onion rings
2 teaspoons neutral high-heat oil, such as avocado, grapeseed, canola, or vegetable oil, for the grill or pan
3/4 cup uncooked short-grain white rice, cooked for serving
1 head red leaf lettuce, leaves separated, optional for wraps
instructions: Place the 1 1/2 pounds flanken-cut beef short ribs in a large bowl of cold water for 30 minutes, changing the water once after 15 minutes if it looks very cloudy.
Drain the 1 1/2 pounds short ribs, then rinse each strip briefly under cold running water to remove bone dust from the cross-cut bones.
Pat the rinsed 1 1/2 pounds short ribs very dry with paper towels and arrange them on a rimmed baking sheet.
Trim away only any thick loose knobs of surface fat from the short ribs, leaving the thin marbling and small fat edges attached for flavor and browning.
Finely grate enough peeled pear to measure 1/3 cup packed pear and juice.
Cut the yellow onion in half, finely grate one half until you have about 3 tablespoons onion and juice, and slice the remaining half into 1/2-inch rings for grilling.

In a mixing bowl, whisk together the 1/3 cup grated pear and juice, 3 tablespoons grated onion and juice, 1/3 cup low-sodium soy sauce, 2 tablespoons packed light brown sugar, 1 tablespoon honey, 1 1/2 tablespoons mirin or rice wine, 1 1/2 teaspoons rice vinegar, 1 teaspoon gochujang, 1 1/2 teaspoons toasted sesame oil, 4 finely grated garlic cloves, 1 1/2 teaspoons finely grated ginger, 3/4 teaspoon black pepper, 2 thinly sliced green onions, and 1 1/2 teaspoons toasted sesame seeds.
Before any raw beef touches the marinade, spoon 1/4 cup of the clean marinade into a small saucepan and stir in the remaining 1/2 teaspoon toasted sesame oil.

Bring the 1/4 cup reserved clean marinade to a simmer over medium heat for 2 to 3 minutes, stirring often, until it thickens slightly into a glossy glaze; remove it from the heat and save it for brushing the cooked galbi.
Add the dried short ribs to the mixing bowl with the remaining marinade.
Use clean hands or tongs to turn and massage the short ribs in the marinade for 1 to 2 minutes, making sure the pear, onion, garlic, and scallions coat both sides of every strip.
Press the short ribs down into the marinade, cover the bowl, and refrigerate for at least 6 hours and up to 24 hours. For the deepest flavor with a clean beef texture, aim for 12 to 18 hours.
Take the marinated short ribs out of the refrigerator 30 minutes before cooking so the chill comes off while the meat stays cool.

While the short ribs sit at room temperature, cook the 3/4 cup short-grain white rice if serving with rice, separate the leaves from the red leaf lettuce if using, and put the 8 ounces kimchi in a serving bowl if using.
Brush the sliced half onion rings with about 1/2 teaspoon neutral high-heat oil and season them with a small pinch Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
For an outdoor grill, heat the grill to medium-high, about 450 to 500 degrees F, then clean and oil the grates with about 1 teaspoon neutral high-heat oil.
For indoor cooking, heat a large cast-iron skillet, carbon-steel skillet, or grill pan over medium-high heat for 4 to 5 minutes, then add 1/2 teaspoon neutral high-heat oil and work in batches.
Lift the short ribs from the marinade, let excess marinade drip back into the bowl for 3 to 5 seconds, and lay only as many ribs on the grill or pan as will fit in a single uncrowded layer.
Cook the first side of the short ribs for 2 to 3 minutes, until the edges are browned, the meat releases easily, and dark grill marks or seared patches appear.

Flip the short ribs with tongs and cook the second side for 1 1/2 to 3 minutes, until the meat is sizzling, caramelized, and at least 145 degrees F in the thickest meaty section if checking with a thermometer.
Brush each cooked side lightly with the simmered reserved glaze during only the last 30 to 45 seconds of cooking so the honey and pear turn shiny without burning.

Transfer the cooked short ribs to a clean wire rack or platter and repeat with the remaining marinated short ribs, adding another 1 teaspoon neutral oil to the pan between indoor batches if the surface looks dry.
Grill or sear the oiled half onion rings for 2 to 3 minutes per side, until softened and browned at the edges.
Discard the raw marinade left in the large bowl after the short ribs are removed.
Brush the hot galbi with any remaining simmered reserved glaze, then sprinkle it with the remaining 1 1/2 teaspoons toasted sesame seeds and the remaining thinly sliced green onion.
Rest the galbi for 3 to 5 minutes so the glaze settles and the meat juices stop bubbling.
Serve the galbi immediately with the cooked short-grain rice, grilled onion rings, separated red leaf lettuce leaves, and kimchi if using.
notes: Ask for flanken-cut beef short ribs, also sold as LA galbi or cross-cut short ribs. They should be sliced across the bones about 1/4 to 1/2 inch thick, not English-cut short ribs.
The 30 minute cold-water soak and quick rinse help remove bone dust. Dry the ribs well afterward so the grill browns instead of steaming.
Asian pear adds sweetness and helps tenderize the beef. Bosc pear is a good substitute and is gentler than kiwi or pineapple.
Use low-sodium soy sauce. If using regular soy sauce, reduce the soy sauce to 1/2 cup and add 2 tablespoons water to keep the galbi from tasting too salty after marinating.
The reserved glaze must be taken out before raw beef touches the marinade. Do not brush cooked galbi with leftover raw marinade.
A 12 to 18 hour marinade gives the best balance of seasoning and texture. Going beyond 24 hours can make the surface too soft because of the pear.
Medium-high heat matters. Galbi should cook quickly enough to char the edges while the thin short ribs stay juicy.
Brush the glaze only at the end of cooking because the pear, honey, and brown sugar can burn if they sit over direct heat too long.
If cooking indoors, turn on the vent fan, open a window if needed, and cook in small batches so the ribs sear instead of steaming.
Cut between the bones with kitchen shears at the table or before serving if you want smaller pieces for rice bowls or lettuce wraps.
Leftovers keep for 3 days in the refrigerator. Reheat quickly in a hot skillet with 1 tablespoon water, just until warmed through.
title: Korean BBQ Galbi
totalTime: 55 minutes plus 30 minutes short-rib soak and 6 to 24 hours marinating

author: Robert Carr
description: A hot oven-finished meatball sub with tender beef-pork meatballs, quick tomato sauce, garlic-Parmesan toasted rolls, provolone, mozzarella, and fresh basil.
equipment: Medium saucepan or small Dutch oven

groceryIngredients.item: Italian sub roll or sturdy hoagie roll

ingredients: 1 1/2 ounces Parmesan cheese, finely grated to make about 1/2 cup, divided
1 tablespoon finely chopped fresh flat-leaf parsley
1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt or 3/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt, divided
1/2 teaspoon black pepper, freshly ground if possible, divided
2 tablespoons fresh basil leaves, torn, divided
2 Italian sub rolls or sturdy hoagie rolls, 6 to 8 inches long, split
2 ounces low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella cheese, shredded, about 1/2 cup
instructions: Move an oven rack 6 inches below the broiler and heat the broiler on high.
Line a rimmed baking sheet with aluminum foil and brush the foil with 2 teaspoons extra-virgin olive oil.
In a mixing bowl, stir together 1/4 cup panko breadcrumbs and 2 tablespoons whole milk.
Let the panko and milk sit for 5 minutes so the breadcrumbs soften into a panade.

Add 8 ounces 80/20 ground beef, 4 ounces ground pork, 1 large egg yolk, 1/4 cup finely grated Parmesan cheese, 1 finely grated garlic clove, 1 tablespoon finely chopped parsley, 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, 1/4 teaspoon black pepper, 1/2 teaspoon dried oregano, 1/4 teaspoon lightly crushed fennel seeds, and 1/2 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce to the softened panko.
Mix the meatball mixture gently with your hands just until the beef, pork, egg, cheese, garlic, parsley, and seasonings are evenly distributed.
Divide the meatball mixture into 8 meatballs, using about 2 tablespoons mixture for each meatball.
Roll each meatball lightly so it holds together without being packed tight, then place the 8 meatballs on the oiled foil.
Broil the meatballs for 7 to 9 minutes, rotating the baking sheet once, until the tops are browned in spots; the centers do not need to be fully cooked yet.
While the meatballs broil, warm the remaining 1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil in a medium saucepan or small Dutch oven over medium heat.
Add the finely diced yellow onion, 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/8 teaspoon black pepper to the hot oil.
Cook the onion for 5 to 6 minutes, stirring often, until softened and lightly golden at the edges.
Stir in 1 minced garlic clove, 1 tablespoon tomato paste, and the remaining 1/4 teaspoon dried oregano.
Cook the garlic and tomato paste for 1 minute, stirring constantly, until the tomato paste darkens slightly and smells savory.
Add the 14.5-ounce can crushed tomatoes, 2 tablespoons water, the remaining 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and the remaining 1/2 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce to the saucepan.
Bring the sauce to a steady simmer over medium heat, stirring the bottom of the pan so the tomato paste dissolves into the crushed tomatoes.
Transfer the browned meatballs and any juices from the foil into the simmering sauce.
Reduce the heat to medium-low, partially cover the saucepan, and simmer the meatballs in the sauce for 10 to 12 minutes.
Gently turn the meatballs once halfway through simmering so all sides are coated in sauce.
Check that the center of the largest meatball reaches 160 degrees F on an instant-read thermometer.
Stir 1 tablespoon finely grated Parmesan cheese, 1/2 teaspoon red wine vinegar, and 1 tablespoon torn fresh basil into the sauce.
Reduce the sauce to low heat so the meatballs stay hot while the rolls toast.
Discard the first sheet of foil from the baking sheet, line the baking sheet with fresh foil, and place the 2 split sub rolls cut-side up on the clean foil.
In a small bowl, stir together 1 tablespoon softened unsalted butter, the remaining 1 finely grated garlic clove, and 1 tablespoon finely grated Parmesan cheese.
Spread the garlic-Parmesan butter evenly over the cut sides of the 2 split rolls, using about 3/4 teaspoon butter mixture on each cut side.
Broil the buttered rolls for 1 to 2 minutes, watching constantly, until the cut sides are lightly toasted around the edges.
Move the 2 toasted top rolls to a plate and leave the 2 toasted bottom rolls cut-side up on the baking sheet.
Place 1 slice provolone cheese on each toasted bottom roll.
Use a slotted spoon to place 4 hot meatballs on each bottom roll.
Spoon about 1/3 cup hot tomato sauce over the meatballs on each sandwich.
Tear the remaining 2 slices provolone cheese over the sauced meatballs, dividing 1 slice provolone per sandwich.
Sprinkle 1/4 cup shredded low-moisture mozzarella cheese and 1 tablespoon finely grated Parmesan cheese over each open sandwich.
Broil the open sandwiches for 2 to 3 minutes, until the provolone and mozzarella are melted and lightly browned in spots.
Scatter the remaining 1 tablespoon torn fresh basil over the hot cheese.
Close the sandwiches with the toasted top rolls and serve immediately while the meatballs, sauce, and cheese are hot.
notes: This sandwich is hot by temperature, not spice; the flavor comes from browning, garlic, Parmesan, tomato paste, Worcestershire sauce, fennel, basil, and melted cheese.
Broiling the meatballs first gives fast browned edges, then the sauce finishes them gently so they stay juicy.
Use 80/20 ground beef and ground pork. Very lean beef can make the meatballs firm before the sauce has time to season them.
Mix the meatball mixture gently and stop as soon as it looks even. Overmixed meatballs can turn springy.
Lightly crushed fennel seeds give the meatballs a subtle Italian sausage flavor without needing store-bought sausage.
The rolls should be sturdy enough to hold hot sauce. Soft sandwich rolls work if they are toasted well before filling.
If the sauce bubbles aggressively after the meatballs are added, lower the heat so the meatballs simmer instead of bouncing around and breaking.
The final broil should be quick. Pull the sandwiches as soon as the cheese melts and browns in spots so the bread stays crisp instead of drying out.
Serve right away with extra tomato sauce on the side for spooning over each bite.
title: Skillet Meatball Subs

author: Robert Carr
description: A hot Italian sausage sub with homemade sweet Italian-style pork patties, browned peppers and onions, quick tomato pan sauce, a garlic-toasted roll, provolone, mozzarella, Parmesan, and basil.
equipment: Cast-iron skillet or heavy saute pan

groceryIngredients.item: Italian sub roll or sturdy hoagie roll

ingredients: 1/2 ounce Parmesan cheese, finely grated to make about 3 tablespoons, divided
2 teaspoons finely chopped fresh flat-leaf parsley
3/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt or 1/2 teaspoon Morton kosher salt, divided, plus more to taste
1/2 teaspoon black pepper, freshly ground if possible, divided
1/2 cup crushed tomatoes from a 14.5-ounce can
2 tablespoons water, plus more if needed
1 tablespoon fresh basil leaves, torn, divided
1 Italian sub roll or sturdy hoagie roll, 6 to 8 inches long, split
1 ounce low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella cheese, shredded, about 1/4 cup
instructions: Move an oven rack 6 inches below the broiler.
Line a rimmed baking sheet with aluminum foil.

In a mixing bowl, combine 6 ounces ground pork, 1 tablespoon finely grated Parmesan cheese, 1/2 garlic clove, finely grated, 2 teaspoons finely chopped parsley, 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, 1/4 teaspoon black pepper, 1/2 teaspoon lightly crushed fennel seeds, 1/4 teaspoon dried oregano, 1/4 teaspoon garlic powder, 1/8 teaspoon onion powder, 1/8 teaspoon sweet paprika, a pinch red pepper flakes if using, 1/2 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce, 1/2 teaspoon red wine vinegar, and 1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil.
Mix the pork with your hands for 45 to 60 seconds, just until the Parmesan, garlic, herbs, spices, Worcestershire sauce, vinegar, and oil are evenly distributed and the mixture feels slightly tacky.
Divide the seasoned pork into 2 equal portions, using about 3 ounces pork mixture for each portion.
Shape each portion into a flat 4-inch-long, 1/2-inch-thick oval patty so both patties will fit neatly in the sub roll.
Let the shaped pork patties rest at room temperature for 10 minutes so the salt, spices, and fennel hydrate before browning.
Heat a cast-iron skillet or heavy saute pan over medium-high heat for 2 minutes.
Add 1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil to the hot skillet.
Place the 2 pork patties in the skillet in a single uncrowded layer so they brown instead of steaming.
Cook the pork patties for 3 to 4 minutes on the first side, until well browned.
Flip the pork patties and cook the second side for 3 to 4 minutes, until well browned and the centers reach 160 degrees F.
Transfer the cooked pork patties to a plate and leave about 1 tablespoon fat in the skillet.
Reduce the heat to medium and add the thinly sliced yellow onion, green bell pepper, red bell pepper, a pinch Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and a pinch black pepper to the skillet.
Cook the peppers and onion for 7 to 9 minutes, stirring every minute, until softened, browned at the edges, and sweet-smelling.
Stir in 1 minced garlic clove, 2 teaspoons tomato paste, and the remaining 1/4 teaspoon dried oregano.
Cook the garlic and tomato paste for 1 minute, stirring constantly, until the tomato paste darkens and coats the peppers and onion.
Add 1/2 cup crushed tomatoes, 2 tablespoons water, the remaining 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and the remaining 1/2 teaspoon red wine vinegar.
Bring the tomato-pepper sauce to a simmer over medium heat, scraping the browned pork bits from the bottom of the skillet.
Simmer the tomato-pepper sauce for 4 to 5 minutes, stirring often, until it looks glossy and thick enough to cling to a spoon.
Return the 2 cooked pork patties and any juices from the plate to the skillet.
Spoon tomato-pepper sauce over the pork patties and simmer for 3 to 4 minutes, until the patties are hot and coated.
Stir 2 teaspoons torn fresh basil into the tomato-pepper sauce.
Taste the sauce and add a small pinch of kosher salt or a splash of red wine vinegar only if it needs more seasoning or brightness.
Turn the heat to low so the pork patties and sauce stay hot while the roll toasts.
Heat the broiler on high.
In a small bowl, stir together 1 tablespoon softened unsalted butter, the remaining 1/2 garlic clove, finely grated, and 1 tablespoon finely grated Parmesan cheese.
Place the split sub roll cut-side up on the foil-lined baking sheet.
Spread the garlic-Parmesan butter evenly over the cut sides of the split roll.
Broil the buttered roll for 1 to 2 minutes, watching constantly, until the cut sides are lightly toasted around the edges.
Move the toasted top roll to a plate and leave the toasted bottom roll cut-side up on the baking sheet.
Place 1 slice provolone cheese on the toasted bottom roll.
Place the 2 hot pork patties on the bottom roll.
