Spoon about 1/3 cup hot tomato-pepper sauce over the pork patties.
Tear the remaining 1 slice provolone cheese over the sauced pork patties.
Sprinkle 1/4 cup shredded low-moisture mozzarella cheese and 1 tablespoon finely grated Parmesan cheese over the open sandwich.
Broil the open sandwich for 2 to 3 minutes, until the provolone and mozzarella are melted and lightly browned in spots.
Scatter the remaining 1 teaspoon torn fresh basil over the hot cheese.
Close the sandwich with the toasted top roll and serve immediately while the sausage patties, sauce, peppers, onions, and cheese are hot.
notes: This sandwich is hot by temperature, not spice. The pork is seasoned like sweet Italian sausage with fennel, garlic, oregano, paprika, Parmesan, vinegar, and Worcestershire sauce.
Ground pork keeps this fast because it skips sausage casings while keeping the flavor and texture of an Italian sausage sub.
Use 80/20 ground pork if you can find it. Extra-lean pork can taste dry by the time the cheese melts.
Shape the pork into flat oval patties instead of round meatballs so each bite gets browned pork, peppers, sauce, bread, and cheese.
Mixing the pork until slightly tacky and resting the shaped patties for 10 minutes makes the patties taste more like sausage and less like loose pork burgers.
Browning the pork first builds the pan drippings that season the peppers, onions, and tomato sauce.
Cook the peppers and onion until the edges brown. That sweetness is what keeps the quick sauce from tasting flat.
The red pepper flakes are optional. Skip them for a fully mild sandwich.
The sauce should be thick enough to cling to the patties before assembly. Simmer it 1 to 2 minutes longer if it looks watery.
Use sturdy Italian sub rolls or hoagie rolls. Very soft rolls can collapse under hot sauce unless they are toasted thoroughly.
Watch the broiler closely for both toasting and melting because buttered bread and cheese can go from browned to burned quickly.
Serve immediately while the bread is toasted, the pork is hot, and the cheese is melted.
title: Hot Italian Sausage Sub

author: Robert Carr
description: A hot turkey provolone sandwich with a seared turkey cutlet, garlic-Dijon mayo, mushroom-onion pan sauce, roasted red peppers, dressed arugula, and a toasted ciabatta or hoagie roll.
equipment: Cast-iron skillet or heavy saute pan

groceryIngredients.item: ciabatta roll or sturdy hoagie roll

ingredients: 1 boneless skinless turkey breast cutlet, about 6 ounces
3/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt or 1/2 teaspoon Morton kosher salt, divided
1/2 teaspoon black pepper, freshly ground if possible, divided
2 ounces fresh cremini mushrooms, sliced 1/4 inch thick
1 (12-ounce) jar roasted red peppers, using 2 tablespoons drained strips and 1/2 teaspoon pepper brine
1 ciabatta roll or sturdy hoagie roll, split
1/2 ounce baby arugula, about 1/2 packed cup
instructions: Move an oven rack 6 inches below the broiler and heat the broiler on high.
Line a rimmed baking sheet with aluminum foil.
Pat the turkey breast cutlet very dry with paper towels.
If the turkey cutlet is thicker than 1/2 inch, place it between sheets of parchment or plastic wrap and pound it to an even 1/2 inch thickness.
Season both sides of the turkey cutlet with 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, 1/8 teaspoon black pepper, 1/4 teaspoon smoked paprika, 1/8 teaspoon garlic powder, and 1/4 teaspoon dried thyme.
Sprinkle 2 tablespoons all-purpose flour over a plate.
Press the seasoned turkey cutlet lightly into the flour on both sides, then shake off the excess flour so only a thin coating remains.

Make the garlic-Dijon mayo by stirring together 2 tablespoons mayonnaise, 1 teaspoon Dijon mustard, 1/2 garlic clove, finely grated, 1/4 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce, 1/2 teaspoon red wine vinegar, 1/2 teaspoon roasted red pepper brine, a tiny pinch Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and a pinch black pepper in a small bowl.
Refrigerate the garlic-Dijon mayo until assembly.
Heat a cast-iron skillet or heavy saute pan over medium-high heat for 2 minutes.
Add 1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil to the hot skillet.
Add the floured turkey cutlet.
Cook the turkey cutlet for 2 to 3 minutes on the first side, until golden brown.
Flip the turkey cutlet with tongs and cook the second side for 2 to 3 minutes, until golden brown and the thickest part reaches 160 degrees F.
Transfer the cooked turkey cutlet to a plate.
Loosely cover the cooked turkey cutlet with foil while making the pan sauce.
Reduce the skillet heat to medium and add 1/2 tablespoon unsalted butter.
Add the 2 ounces sliced cremini mushrooms, the thinly sliced yellow onion, a pinch Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and a pinch black pepper.
Cook the mushrooms and onion for 7 to 8 minutes, stirring every minute, until the mushrooms are browned, the onion is softened, and most of the moisture has cooked off.
Stir in 1 minced garlic clove and the remaining 1/4 teaspoon dried thyme.
Cook the garlic and thyme for 30 seconds, stirring constantly, until fragrant.
Add 2 tablespoons low-sodium chicken broth, the remaining 1/4 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce, and 1/2 teaspoon red wine vinegar.
Simmer the mushroom-onion pan sauce for 2 to 3 minutes, scraping the browned turkey bits from the skillet, until the liquid reduces by about half and coats the mushrooms and onions.
Return the turkey cutlet and any juices from the plate to the skillet.
Turn the turkey cutlet in the mushroom-onion pan sauce for 30 seconds so it is coated and hot.
Turn the heat to low so the turkey and mushroom-onion pan sauce stay warm while the roll toasts.
In a small bowl, stir together the remaining 1/2 tablespoon softened unsalted butter, the remaining 1/2 garlic clove, finely grated, and a tiny pinch Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
Place the split ciabatta roll or hoagie roll cut-side up on the foil-lined baking sheet.
Spread the garlic butter evenly over the cut sides of the split roll.
Broil the buttered roll for 1 to 2 minutes, watching constantly, until the cut sides are lightly toasted around the edges.
Move the toasted top roll to a plate and leave the toasted bottom roll cut-side up on the baking sheet.
Spread about 1 tablespoon garlic-Dijon mayo on the toasted bottom roll.
Place 1 slice provolone cheese on the mayo-coated bottom roll.
Place the hot turkey cutlet on the bottom roll, folding or trimming the turkey as needed so it fits the bread.
Spoon the mushroom-onion pan sauce over the turkey cutlet.
Add about 2 tablespoons drained roasted red pepper strips to the sandwich.
Tear the remaining 1 slice provolone cheese over the roasted red peppers.
Broil the open sandwich for 2 to 3 minutes, until the provolone is melted and lightly browned in spots.

While the cheese melts, toss 1/2 ounce baby arugula with the remaining 1/2 teaspoon red wine vinegar, the remaining 1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil, a tiny pinch Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and a pinch black pepper.
Top the hot sandwich with the dressed arugula.
Spread about 1 tablespoon garlic-Dijon mayo on the toasted top roll.
Close the sandwich and serve immediately while the turkey, mushroom-onion pan sauce, and provolone are hot.
notes: This sandwich is hot by temperature, not spice; the flavor comes from browned turkey, mushroom-onion pan sauce, roasted red peppers, garlic-Dijon mayo, toasted bread, and melted provolone.
Turkey breast cutlets cook quickly; for this version, one cutlet keeps the recipe compact.
Pounding any thick turkey pieces to 1/2 inch helps them cook evenly before the flour coating over-browns.
The thin flour coating gives the turkey better browning and helps the chicken broth reduce into a light pan sauce instead of tasting watery.
Use low-sodium chicken broth so the pan sauce can reduce without becoming too salty.
Roasted red pepper brine seasons the mayo with a little sweetness and acidity; use the liquid from the jar, not extra vinegar.
If cooking more than one turkey cutlet, do not crowd the skillet. Crowding traps steam and prevents browning.
The turkey is pulled at 160 degrees F because it will stay hot in the skillet and under the broiler during assembly.
Dress the arugula right before serving so it stays peppery and fresh against the hot turkey and cheese.
Serve immediately while the bread is crisp, the turkey is hot, and the provolone is melted.
title: Hot Turkey Provolone Sandwich

additionalTime: 10 minutes tomato and onion seasoning time plus 5 minutes sandwich pressing
author: Robert Carr
description: A cold Italian grinder sandwich with thin-sliced ham, Genoa salami, capicola, provolone, garlic-oregano mayo, seasoned tomatoes and onions, chopped giardiniera, pepperoncini, and a red wine vinaigrette grinder salad.

groceryIngredients.item: Italian sub roll or sturdy hoagie roll

groceryIngredients.display: paper-thin hot capicola or deli ham
iceberg lettuce or shredded iceberg lettuce

ingredients: 1 Italian sub roll or sturdy hoagie roll, 6 to 7 inches long, split but not fully separated
1 1/2 ounces hot capicola or additional deli ham, sliced paper-thin
1 small ripe tomato, sliced 1/4 inch thick
A few very thin slices red onion
1 (12-ounce) jar sliced pepperoncini peppers, using 2 tablespoons peppers and 2 teaspoons pepperoncini brine
1 (12-ounce) jar mild or hot giardiniera, using 2 tablespoons drained and finely chopped vegetables
1 small garlic clove, finely grated or minced to a paste
1/4 ounce Parmesan cheese, finely grated, about 2 tablespoons loosely packed, divided
1/8 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt or a small pinch Morton kosher salt, divided
1/4 teaspoon black pepper, freshly ground if possible, divided
instructions: Place the sliced tomato and red onion in a shallow bowl or on a plate.
Drizzle the tomato and onion with 1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil.
Season the tomato and onion with a small pinch of Diamond Crystal kosher salt and a pinch of black pepper.
Turn the tomatoes and onion gently with your hands or tongs until the oil, salt, and pepper coat both sides of the tomato slices.
Let the seasoned tomatoes and onion sit for 10 minutes while making the mayo and grinder salad.

Make the garlic-oregano mayo in a small bowl by stirring together 3 tablespoons mayonnaise, 1 finely grated small garlic clove, 1 teaspoon Dijon mustard, 1 teaspoon red wine vinegar, 1 teaspoon pepperoncini brine, 1 tablespoon finely grated Parmesan cheese, 1/4 teaspoon dried oregano, a pinch of garlic powder, a tiny pinch of Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and a pinch of black pepper.
Taste the garlic-oregano mayo; it should be tangy, salty, garlicky, and slightly sharp from the vinegar and pepperoncini brine.
Refrigerate the garlic-oregano mayo while preparing the salad.
Drain 2 tablespoons sliced pepperoncini peppers and 2 tablespoons giardiniera well so the finished sandwich stays crisp instead of watery.
Finely chop the 2 tablespoons drained giardiniera into pieces about 1/4 inch wide.

Make the grinder salad dressing in a medium bowl by whisking together the remaining 1 teaspoon red wine vinegar, remaining 1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil, remaining 1 teaspoon pepperoncini brine, remaining 1/4 teaspoon dried oregano, remaining garlic powder, 1/8 teaspoon onion powder, a pinch of red pepper flakes if using, a tiny pinch of Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and the remaining black pepper.
Add the 1 cup finely shredded iceberg lettuce, 2 tablespoons drained sliced pepperoncini peppers, and 2 tablespoons finely chopped giardiniera to the bowl with the dressing.
Toss the grinder salad for 30 to 45 seconds, until every strand of lettuce is lightly coated and the pepperoncini and giardiniera are evenly distributed.
Open the split Italian sub roll or hoagie roll without separating the hinge.
If the roll is very thick, pinch out a shallow strip of bread from the inside of the top roll so the sandwich closes around the filling.
Spread about 1 tablespoon garlic-oregano mayo on the inside bottom half of the roll.
Spread about 1 tablespoon garlic-oregano mayo on the inside top half of the roll.
Place 1 slice provolone cheese on the bottom half of the mayo-coated roll.
Press 1 slice provolone cheese onto the inside top half of the mayo-coated roll so both sides of the bread have a cheese barrier.
Fold and layer 2 ounces paper-thin smoked deli ham over the bottom provolone.
Fold and layer 1 1/2 ounces paper-thin Genoa salami over the ham.
Fold and layer 1 1/2 ounces paper-thin hot capicola or additional deli ham over the salami.
Lift the seasoned tomato slices and red onion from their bowl, leaving extra tomato juices behind.
Place the seasoned tomato and red onion on the sandwich.
Sprinkle the remaining finely grated Parmesan cheese over the tomatoes and onion.
Pile the dressed grinder salad onto the sandwich.
Close the sandwich firmly so the dressed lettuce compresses into the meats and cheese.
Wrap the closed sandwich tightly in parchment paper or aluminum foil.
Press the wrapped sandwich under a cutting board, sheet pan, or heavy skillet for 5 minutes so the roll absorbs the mayo and vinaigrette without falling apart.
Cut the cold Italian grinder sandwich in half through the wrapper, then unwrap and serve immediately.
notes: This is a no-cook sandwich, so the flavor comes from strong seasoning: garlic-oregano mayo, red wine vinegar, pepperoncini brine, chopped giardiniera, salty provolone, and seasoned tomatoes.
Ask the deli counter for the ham, salami, and capicola sliced paper-thin. Very thin slices fold into layers and are easier to bite through.
Use 1 garlic clove in the mayo if the cloves are very large, very fresh, or sharply spicy. Use 2 small to medium cloves when you want a stronger garlic bite.
Use hot capicola and hot giardiniera for a spicier sandwich, or use extra deli ham and mild giardiniera for a milder version.
Do not skip the 10-minute tomato and onion seasoning step. Cold raw tomatoes and onions need salt, pepper, and olive oil to taste finished inside a sandwich.
Drain the pepperoncini and giardiniera before adding them to the lettuce. Too much jar liquid makes the rolls soggy before the first bite.
Dress the iceberg lettuce right before assembly so it stays crisp but still picks up the oregano vinaigrette.
The recipe uses a restrained amount of salt because the deli meats, provolone, Parmesan, pepperoncini, and giardiniera already bring plenty of salt.
Use sturdy Italian sub rolls or hoagie rolls with a hinge. Very soft sandwich bread can tear once the grinder salad and seasoned tomatoes go in.
Splitting the provolone between the bottom and top of each roll helps protect the bread from the seasoned tomatoes, onion, and dressed grinder salad.
The 5-minute wrapped press helps the sandwich hold together and lets the mayo, vinaigrette, meat, cheese, tomato, onion, and salad combine.

This sandwich is best right after pressing. For make-ahead, keep the garlic-oregano mayo, seasoned tomatoes and onions, and undressed lettuce mixture separate, then dress and assemble within 15 minutes of serving.
title: Cold Italian Grinder Sandwich

additionalTime: 10 minutes dried chile soak plus 15 minutes flavor rest
author: Robert Carr
description: Roasted red taqueria salsa with Roma tomatoes, tomatillos, jalapenos, guajillo, a small amount of chile de arbol, garlic, onion, lime, vinegar, and a brief hot-oil simmer.
ingredients: 1 dried guajillo chile, stemmed and seeded
2 dried chile de arbol peppers, stemmed
1 tablespoon avocado oil for frying the salsa
1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, or 1/2 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1 to 3 tablespoons chile soaking water or fresh hot water, only as needed
instructions: Toast 1 stemmed and seeded dried guajillo chile and 2 stemmed dried chile de arbol peppers in a dry skillet over medium heat for 15 to 30 seconds per side, just until fragrant and slightly softened, not scorched.
Place the toasted chiles in a small bowl, cover with very hot water, and soak for 10 minutes.
While the chiles soak, place 5 Roma tomatoes, 3 tomatillos, 2 jalapenos, 1/2 small white onion, and 4 unpeeled garlic cloves in a dry cast-iron skillet or comal over medium-high heat.
Roast the vegetables for 10 to 14 minutes, turning occasionally, until the tomatoes and tomatillos soften, the jalapenos blister, the onion chars in spots, and the garlic is browned and soft inside.
Peel the roasted garlic cloves.
Drain the soaked chiles, reserving the soaking water.
Taste the reserved chile soaking water. If it tastes bitter or harsh, use fresh hot water for thinning instead.

Add the soaked chiles to a blender with the roasted tomatoes, tomatillos, jalapenos, onion, peeled garlic, 1 tablespoon apple cider vinegar, 1 tablespoon fresh lime juice, 1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, 1/4 teaspoon black pepper, and 1/2 teaspoon honey if using.
Blend on medium speed for 20 to 35 seconds, using a tamper only if your blender needs help moving, until the salsa is smooth but not whipped or foamy.
For a thinner taqueria-style texture, blend in 1 to 3 tablespoons chile soaking water or fresh hot water, 1 tablespoon at a time.
For a smoother taqueria texture, pass the blended salsa through a fine-mesh strainer; for a lightly rustic table salsa texture, leave it unstrained.
Heat 1 tablespoon avocado oil in a small saucepan over medium heat until shimmering.
Carefully pour the blended salsa into the hot oil; it may splatter at first.
Simmer over medium-low heat for 3 to 5 minutes, stirring often, until the raw sharpness rounds off, the oil looks integrated, and the color deepens slightly.
Taste and adjust only if needed with a small pinch of kosher salt, a few drops of lime juice, or a few drops of honey.
Let the salsa rest for at least 15 minutes before serving so the roasted tomato, chile, and acid flavors settle.
notes: This roja is the everyday red salsa in the set: smoother and more tomato-forward than the hotter guajillo-arbol drip salsa.
If using a Vitamix 5200, start on Variable 1 and increase to Variable 5 or 6. Do not use High unless you intentionally want a very smooth hot-sauce texture.
The tomatillos add brightness and structure, but the salsa should still taste like a red taqueria salsa rather than salsa verde.
Frying the blended salsa briefly in hot oil gives it a rounder taqueria finish than blending the oil in raw.
If the chile soaking water tastes bitter, do not use it. Fresh hot water keeps the salsa cleaner and more tomato-forward.
Use 1 chile de arbol for mild heat, 2 for medium, or 4 for hot.
If any chile smells bitter or looks blackened after toasting, discard it and toast another one. Scorched dried chiles can make the whole batch taste harsh.
This salsa works especially well with carne asada burritos, breakfast burritos, tacos, eggs, chips, and quesadillas.
title: Roasted Tomato-Tomatillo Taqueria Roja Salsa

additionalTime: 10 minutes poblano steam time, 5 minutes cooling time, plus 20 minutes flavor rest
author: Robert Carr
description: Roasted green salsa with deeply broiled tomatillos, poblano, jalapeno, serrano, garlic, onion, cilantro, lime, and a small amount of honey.
equipment: Bowl or foil for steaming poblano
ingredients: 1 serrano pepper, optional for extra heat
1/2 small white onion, cut into 2 wedges, plus 2 tablespoons finely chopped white onion for finishing
1/2 cup packed cilantro leaves and tender stems, divided
1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, or 1/2 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1 to 3 tablespoons reserved roasted tomatillo juices or water, only as needed
instructions: Position an oven rack 5 to 6 inches from the broiler, heat the broiler to high, and line a rimmed baking sheet with aluminum foil.
Cut any large tomatillos in half through the equator. Leave small tomatillos whole.
Place 1 1/2 pounds tomatillos, 1 large poblano pepper, 2 jalapenos, 1 serrano pepper if using, 2 white onion wedges, and 4 unpeeled garlic cloves on the prepared baking sheet.
Broil for 6 to 8 minutes, until the tomatillos are blistered on top and starting to collapse and the chile skins are blackened in patches.
Turn the poblano, jalapenos, serrano if using, onion, and garlic. Leave halved tomatillos cut-side down unless they are browning unevenly.

Broil for 4 to 8 minutes more, pulling the garlic early if it is browned and soft, until the tomatillos are olive-green and slumped, the onion is charred at the edges, and the chile skins are well blistered.
Transfer the poblano to a covered bowl or wrap it loosely in foil and let it steam for 10 minutes.
Let the tomatillos, jalapenos, serrano, onion, and garlic cool on the sheet pan for 5 minutes. Do not pour the pan juices into the blender yet.
Rub most of the blackened skin from the poblano, then remove the stem and seeds.
Peel the roasted garlic cloves and remove the stems from the jalapenos and serrano.
For a milder salsa, split the jalapenos and serrano and remove some or all of the seeds and membranes before blending.

Add the roasted tomatillos, peeled poblano, 1 jalapeno, roasted onion wedges, peeled garlic, 1/4 cup cilantro, 1 1/2 tablespoons fresh lime juice, and 3/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt to a blender.
Pulse on low speed until the salsa is evenly chopped but still slightly textured, 8 to 12 short pulses.
Taste for heat. Add the second roasted jalapeno and the serrano if using, a little at a time, blending briefly after each addition, until the salsa tastes as warm as you want it.
If the salsa is too thick to spoon, blend in reserved roasted tomatillo juices or water 1 tablespoon at a time. Stop while it is loose but not watery.
Pulse in the remaining 1/4 cup cilantro just until flecked through the salsa.
Transfer the salsa to a bowl, stir in 2 tablespoons finely chopped white onion, and let it rest for at least 20 minutes.
Taste again after the rest and adjust only if needed with the remaining 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, a few drops of lime juice, or 1/2 teaspoon honey if the tomatillos are very sharp.
notes: The poblano is the upgrade. It adds roasted green chile depth without making the salsa taste heavy or overly spicy.
Broiling the tomatillos until they slump and char in spots concentrates their flavor better than stopping as soon as they soften.
Keep the roasted tomatillo juices out of the blender at first. Tomatillos vary in wateriness, and adding all the juices immediately can make the salsa thin and washed out.
Adding only 1 roasted jalapeno at first makes the heat repeatable. The second jalapeno and optional serrano are there for adjustment, not blind commitment.
Folding in a little raw onion and pulsing in the last cilantro after blending keeps the finished salsa bright.
Do not rinse the poblano after charring. Rinsing washes away roasted flavor.
Black pepper is intentionally omitted because it can muddy the clean tomatillo, chile, cilantro, and lime flavor.
If the salsa tastes too tart, use the optional honey before adding more salt. Tomatillos can vary a lot in sharpness.
If using a Vitamix 5200, low pulses around Variable 2 to 4 keep the salsa rustic, while Variable 6 or 7 makes it smoother.
For a mild salsa verde, skip the serrano and remove the jalapeno seeds before blending.
This salsa works especially well with breakfast burritos, pork, chicken, eggs, grilled vegetables, tacos, and chips.
title: Roasted Poblano Salsa Verde

additionalTime: 10 minutes cooling time plus 20 minutes flavor rest
author: Robert Carr
description: Creamy jalapeno taqueria salsa made by cooking jalapenos, a little white onion, and garlic, then blending them with neutral oil, lime, a little vinegar, cilantro, and salt.
ingredients: 1/2 cup avocado oil or other neutral oil, at room temperature
1 1/4 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt, or 3/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1/4 cup cilantro leaves and tender stems, optional
2 to 4 tablespoons cooled jalapeno cooking water, only as needed
instructions: Place 10 stemmed jalapenos and 1/4 small white onion in a medium saucepan and cover with water by 1 inch.
Bring to a simmer over medium-high heat, then reduce the heat to maintain a steady simmer and cook for 6 minutes.
Add 4 peeled garlic cloves and simmer for 2 to 4 minutes more, until the jalapenos turn olive-green, the onion is softened, and the garlic is just tender.
Use a slotted spoon to transfer the jalapenos, onion, and garlic to a plate and let them cool for 10 minutes. Reserve the cooking water and let it cool slightly.
For a milder sauce, split the cooked jalapenos and remove some or all of the seeds and membranes before blending.
Add the cooled jalapenos, onion, garlic, 1 tablespoon fresh lime juice, 1 teaspoon white vinegar, 1 1/4 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/4 cup cilantro if using to a blender.
Blend on medium speed until the jalapenos are fully broken down into a thick, smooth puree, 30 to 45 seconds, scraping the blender jar if needed.

With the blender running on medium to medium-high, slowly stream in 1/2 cup room-temperature avocado oil through the lid opening, then blend on high for 20 to 30 seconds until the salsa turns pale, creamy, and glossy.
If the oil starts to pool while streaming, pause the oil, blend until the salsa looks glossy again, then continue streaming slowly.
If the salsa is too thick to pour, blend in cooled reserved jalapeno cooking water 1 tablespoon at a time until it is spoonable and lightly drippy.
Taste and adjust only if needed with a small pinch of kosher salt or a few drops of lime juice.
Let the salsa rest for at least 20 minutes before serving.
notes: This salsa is creamy because the cooked jalapenos blend smoothly with oil. It does not contain dairy.
The small amount of cooked white onion rounds the jalapeno flavor without making the salsa taste oniony.
Adding the garlic near the end keeps it tender and savory without washing out all of its bite.
A high-powered blender such as a Vitamix 5200 makes the smoothest texture, but a regular blender works if you stream the oil slowly and blend a little longer.
If using a Vitamix 5200, blend the chile base around Variable 5 to 6 until smooth, then stream the oil in slowly before finishing briefly on High.
Room-temperature oil and cooled chiles make the emulsion more stable. Very hot chiles can trap steam and make the sauce thin or unstable.
Keep the acid restrained. Too much lime or vinegar makes creamy jalapeno salsa taste sharp instead of green and rich.
Avocado oil keeps the flavor clean. Canola or grapeseed oil also works. Olive oil is not ideal here because it can turn bitter and assertive when blended hard.
For a mild version, remove most of the jalapeno seeds after cooking. For a hotter version, leave the seeds in or replace 2 jalapenos with serranos.
Cilantro is optional. Use it for a greener, fresher sauce; skip it for the cleaner taqueria-style jalapeno-and-garlic flavor.
Do not add the oil all at once. Streaming it in helps the oil blend into the sauce instead of floating on top.
Use the cooking water only after the oil has been blended in. Adding too much water early makes the salsa loose instead of creamy.
The salsa thickens slightly after resting and again after chilling. Stir before serving, and thin with a small splash of water only if needed.
This salsa works especially well on breakfast burritos, tacos, potatoes, grilled chicken, eggs, quesadillas, and rice bowls.
title: Creamy Jalapeño Taqueria Salsa

author: Robert Carr
description: Fresh table salsa with whole peeled tomatoes, fire-roasted tomatoes, jalapeno, white onion, garlic, cilantro, lime, cumin, salt, and a short low-speed pulse technique that keeps it lightly chunky.
ingredients: 1 28-ounce can whole peeled tomatoes, drained with 1/2 cup juice reserved
1 jalapeno, stemmed, seeded for mild salsa or left with seeds for medium heat, and roughly chopped
1/3 cup roughly chopped white onion, rinsed and drained if very sharp
1/2 cup cilantro leaves and tender stems
1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, or 1/2 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
instructions: Drain 1 28-ounce can whole peeled tomatoes, reserving 1/2 cup of the juice, and drain 1 14.5-ounce can fire-roasted tomatoes.
If the chopped onion tastes very sharp, rinse it under cold water for 10 seconds and drain well.

Add 1 jalapeno, 1/3 cup roughly chopped white onion, 1 roughly chopped garlic clove, 1/2 cup cilantro, 2 tablespoons fresh lime juice, 1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt or 1/2 teaspoon Morton kosher salt, 1/2 teaspoon cumin, 1/4 teaspoon black pepper, and 1/2 teaspoon granulated sugar if using to a blender.
Add about half of the drained tomatoes over the jalapeno, onion, garlic, and cilantro.
Pulse on low speed 2 to 4 times, just until the jalapeno, onion, garlic, and cilantro are chopped.
Add the remaining drained tomatoes and pulse on low speed 3 to 6 more times, stopping while the salsa still has small tomato pieces and a loose table-salsa texture.
If the salsa is thicker than you like, stir in reserved tomato juice 1 tablespoon at a time with a rubber spatula instead of blending longer.
Transfer the salsa to a bowl, stir, taste, and adjust only if needed with a small pinch of kosher salt, a few drops of lime juice, or a pinch of sugar.
Cover and rest for 45 minutes so the canned tomato, onion, cilantro, lime, and cumin flavors come together.
After resting, stir and taste once more before serving, adding only a small pinch of kosher salt or a few drops of lime juice if the salsa tastes flat.
notes: Whole peeled tomatoes give a better blended texture than using only diced tomatoes. The fire-roasted tomatoes add charred depth without making the salsa taste fully cooked.
This salsa needs restraint in a powerful blender. If using a Vitamix 5200, pulse on Variable 2 or 3 in very short bursts instead of blending continuously.
Do not fix thickness by blending longer. Stir in reserved tomato juice instead so the salsa stays fresh and lightly chunky.
For a mild salsa, remove the jalapeno seeds and white ribs. For a hotter salsa, use 2 jalapenos or add 1 serrano.
The sugar is optional and should only be used if the tomatoes taste flat or metallic.
This salsa works especially well with chips, tacos, eggs, rice bowls, grilled meats, and simple weeknight meals.
title: Fresh Table Salsa

additionalTime: 15 minutes dried chile soak plus 15 minutes flavor rest
author: Robert Carr
description: Smooth simmered guajillo-arbol salsa for spooning over burrito bites, with roasted tomatoes, tomatillos, garlic, onion, toasted dried chiles, a little vinegar and lime, honey, and a hot-oil finish.
ingredients: 2 dried guajillo chiles, stemmed and seeded
4 dried chile de arbol peppers, stemmed
1/2 cup chile soaking water or fresh hot water, plus more as needed
1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, or 1/2 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
instructions: Toast 2 stemmed and seeded dried guajillo chiles and 4 stemmed dried chile de arbol peppers in a dry skillet over medium heat for 15 to 30 seconds per side, just until fragrant and slightly softened, not blackened.
Place the toasted chiles in a small bowl, cover with very hot water, and soak for 15 minutes.
While the chiles soak, place 3 Roma tomatoes, 2 tomatillos, 1/4 small white onion, and 4 unpeeled garlic cloves in a dry cast-iron skillet or comal over medium-high heat.
Roast for 10 to 14 minutes, turning occasionally, until the tomatoes and tomatillos soften, the onion chars in spots, and the garlic is browned and soft inside.
Peel the roasted garlic cloves.
Drain the soaked chiles, reserving the soaking water.
Taste the reserved chile soaking water. If it tastes bitter or harsh, use fresh hot water for blending and thinning instead.

Add the soaked chiles, roasted tomatoes, tomatillos, onion, peeled garlic, 1/2 cup chile soaking water or fresh hot water, 1 1/2 tablespoons apple cider vinegar, 1 tablespoon fresh lime juice, 1 teaspoon honey, 1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/2 teaspoon black pepper to a blender.
Blend on high for 35 to 60 seconds until very smooth.
For the silkiest drip texture, pass the blended salsa through a fine-mesh strainer; for a slightly rustic sauce, leave it unstrained.
Heat 2 tablespoons avocado oil in a small saucepan over medium heat until shimmering.
Carefully pour the blended salsa into the hot oil; it may splatter at first.
Simmer over medium-low heat for 5 to 7 minutes, stirring often, until the color deepens slightly, the oil looks integrated, and the salsa lightly coats a spoon but still drips easily.
If the salsa gets too thick, whisk in reserved chile soaking water or fresh hot water 1 tablespoon at a time until it is thin enough to drip from a spoon.
Taste and adjust only if needed with a small pinch of kosher salt, a few drops of lime juice, or a few drops of honey.
Let the salsa rest for 15 minutes before serving.
notes: This salsa is smoother and more chile-forward than the everyday roja, so it works well spooned onto burrito bites.
Four chile de arbol peppers gives a useful medium-hot default. Use 2 for mild, 6 for hot, or 8 for very hot.
Frying the blended salsa in hot oil matters. It makes the dried chile flavor rounder, gives the sauce a light gloss, and softens the sharp vinegar and lime flavor.
If the chile soaking water tastes bitter, do not use it. Fresh hot water gives a cleaner finished salsa.
Aim for a sauce loose enough to drip from a spoon but thick enough to briefly coat eggs, potatoes, steak, or tortilla.
Do not use scorched chiles. A few blackened spots on roasted vegetables are good, but blackened dried chiles taste bitter.
This salsa works especially well with sausage breakfast burritos, carne asada burritos, eggs, potatoes, tacos, quesadillas, and grilled meats.
title: Simmered Guajillo-Arbol Drip Salsa

additionalTime: 10 minutes potato steam-dry time, 10 minutes chile soak, plus 3 to 5 minutes foil rest
author: Robert Carr
description: A focused sausage, egg, potato, and cheese breakfast burrito with crispy Yukon Gold potatoes, deeply browned breakfast sausage, soft scrambled eggs, a three-cheese melt, toasted flour tortillas, and a simmered guajillo-arbol breakfast drip sauce served on the side for adding to each bite.
equipment: 12-inch cast-iron skillet or heavy saute pan
ingredients: 10 ounces Yukon Gold potatoes, cut into 1/2-inch cubes
2 teaspoons apple cider vinegar for the potato water
2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the potato water
1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the potatoes, or 1/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1/2 teaspoon black pepper for the potatoes
6 ounces bulk pork breakfast sausage, preferably sage-forward
1 tablespoon avocado oil for the potatoes, only as needed
1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the eggs, or 1/8 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1/4 teaspoon black pepper for the eggs
2 ounces sharp cheddar cheese, freshly shredded
2 ounces Monterey Jack cheese, freshly shredded
2 large burrito-size flour tortillas, preferably 12 inches
1 dried guajillo chile, stemmed and seeded
1 dried chile de arbol pepper, stem removed
1 tablespoon apple cider vinegar for the hot sauce
2 teaspoons fresh lime juice for the hot sauce
1 tablespoon chile soaking water or fresh hot water, plus more as needed
2 teaspoons avocado oil for the hot sauce
1/2 teaspoon cumin for the hot sauce
1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the hot sauce, or 1/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1/2 teaspoon black pepper for the hot sauce
instructions: Place the 10 ounces diced Yukon Gold potatoes in a medium saucepan and cover them with cold water by 1 inch.
Add 2 teaspoons apple cider vinegar and 2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt to the potato water.
Bring the potatoes to a boil over high heat, then reduce the heat and simmer for 5 to 7 minutes, until the potatoes are barely tender but not falling apart.
Drain the potatoes well, spread them on paper towels or a wire rack, pat the tops dry if they look wet, and let them steam-dry for 10 minutes.
While the potatoes dry, make the simmered guajillo-arbol breakfast drip sauce.

Toast 1 stemmed and seeded dried guajillo chile and 1 stemmed dried chile de arbol pepper in a dry cast-iron skillet over medium heat for 15 to 30 seconds per side, turning often, just until fragrant and slightly softened, not scorched.
Transfer the toasted chiles to a small bowl, cover them with hot water, and soak for 10 minutes.
While the chiles soak, place 2 tomatillos, 1 Roma tomato, 1 jalapeno, 1/8 white onion, and 2 unpeeled garlic cloves in the dry cast-iron skillet over medium-high heat.

Cook the hot sauce vegetables for 8 to 12 minutes, turning occasionally, until the tomatillos soften, the garlic is browned and soft inside, and the tomatoes, jalapenos, and onion are charred in places.
Peel the roasted garlic cloves.
Drain the soaked chiles, reserving the soaking water.
Taste the reserved chile soaking water. If it tastes bitter or harsh, use fresh hot water for blending and thinning instead.
Transfer the soaked chiles to a blender.

Add the roasted tomatillos, tomato, jalapeno, onion, peeled garlic, 1 tablespoon apple cider vinegar, 2 teaspoons fresh lime juice, 1 tablespoon chile soaking water or fresh hot water, 1/2 teaspoon honey, 1/2 teaspoon cumin, 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/2 teaspoon black pepper to the blender.
Blend the hot sauce on high for 35 to 60 seconds until very smooth, adding 1 to 3 tablespoons more chile soaking water or fresh hot water only if needed to make it loose enough to drip from a spoon.
Heat 2 teaspoons avocado oil in a small saucepan over medium heat.
Carefully pour the blended hot sauce into the hot oil; it may splatter at first.
Simmer the hot sauce over medium-low heat for 5 to 7 minutes, stirring often, until the color deepens slightly, the oil looks integrated, and the sauce lightly coats a spoon but still drips easily.
If the hot sauce gets too thick, whisk in chile soaking water or fresh hot water 1 tablespoon at a time until it drips easily again.
Taste the hot sauce and adjust only if needed with a small pinch of kosher salt, a few drops of lime juice, or a few drops of honey.
For a smoother drip sauce, pass the hot sauce through a fine-mesh strainer; for a more rustic sauce, leave it unstrained.

Cook 6 ounces bulk pork breakfast sausage in a large skillet over medium heat for 6 to 8 minutes, breaking it into small crumbles and letting the crumbles sit against the pan long enough to brown, until deeply browned and fully cooked.
Transfer the sausage to a paper towel-lined plate with a slotted spoon.
Reserve 1 tablespoon of sausage fat in the skillet and discard the rest, or add 1 tablespoon avocado oil if the sausage did not release enough fat.

Toss the steam-dried potatoes with 1 1/2 teaspoons cornstarch, 1/2 teaspoon smoked paprika, 1/2 teaspoon garlic powder, 1/4 teaspoon onion powder, 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/2 teaspoon black pepper.
Heat the reserved sausage fat in the skillet over medium-high heat until hot.
Add the seasoned potatoes in a single layer, using a second skillet or working in batches if needed so they are not crowded.
Cook the potatoes, or each batch, undisturbed for 3 minutes, then turn every 2 to 3 minutes for 10 to 14 minutes total, until deeply golden and crisp on multiple sides.
Transfer the crispy potatoes to a plate and keep them uncovered so they stay crisp.
Whisk 4 large eggs with 1 1/2 tablespoons half-and-half, 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/4 teaspoon black pepper until no streaks of egg white remain.
Melt 1 tablespoon unsalted butter in a nonstick skillet or clean well-seasoned skillet over medium-low heat.
Add the eggs and cook gently, folding with a rubber spatula, until the eggs form soft curds and are just set but still slightly looser than you want in the finished burrito.
Immediately transfer the eggs to a plate and spread them into a shallow layer for 1 to 2 minutes so leftover heat slows down and excess steam escapes.
Warm each 12-inch flour tortilla in a dry skillet for 20 to 30 seconds per side, or until soft, flexible, and lightly toasted in spots.
Assemble 1 burrito at a time by placing a warm tortilla on a clean work surface.
Place 1 slice American cheese just below the center of the tortilla.
Sprinkle about 1 ounce sharp cheddar cheese and 1 ounce Monterey Jack cheese over the American cheese.
Add half of the crispy potatoes over the cheese.
Add half of the browned breakfast sausage over the potatoes.
Add half of the soft scrambled eggs over the sausage.
Fold the left and right sides of the tortilla over the filling, then roll tightly from the bottom into a compact burrito.
Place the burrito seam-side down in a dry skillet over medium heat.
Toast the burrito for 1 to 2 minutes per side, starting with the seam side, until lightly crisp, sealed, and browned in spots.
Wrap the toasted burrito in foil and let it rest for 3 to 5 minutes so the cheese finishes melting and the filling settles.
Repeat with the remaining tortillas and fillings.
Serve each burrito with the simmered guajillo-arbol breakfast drip sauce on the side.
Drip a small spoonful of hot sauce onto each bite as you eat instead of pouring it inside the burrito.
notes: This recipe intentionally keeps the burrito filling limited to sausage, egg, potato, and cheese. The hot sauce is served outside the burrito and does not count as an internal filling.

The reduced filling ratio is intentional. Two burritos made with 10 ounces potatoes, 6 ounces sausage, 4 eggs, and the listed cheese are still full, but they roll cleaner and eat with better balance than an overstuffed version.
Bulk pork breakfast sausage is better than sliced sausage links because it browns into small crumbles that distribute evenly through the burrito.
A sage-forward breakfast sausage gives the most classic breakfast flavor. Avoid maple breakfast sausage unless you specifically want a sweeter burrito.
Reserve only enough sausage fat to crisp the potatoes. Too much loose grease makes the burrito heavy.
Yukon Gold potatoes are the best choice because they get creamy inside and crisp outside without turning mealy.
The vinegar in the potato water helps the potato cubes hold their shape while they simmer.
The cornstarch coating is a small but important crispness boost. It helps the potatoes brown and gives them a rough, crisp exterior.
The potatoes should be crisp before they go into the burrito because they soften slightly after the foil rest.
Do not crowd the potatoes in the skillet. A crowded pan steams the potato cubes and prevents the rough cornstarch-coated edges from turning crisp.
Do not overcook the eggs until dry. The target is soft, tender curds with no wet liquid pooling in the pan.
Spreading the eggs on a plate before assembly prevents leftover heat from making them dry and lets extra steam escape before the burrito is wrapped.
Freshly shredded cheddar and Monterey Jack melt better than pre-shredded cheese. The American cheese is included for melt, binding, and burrito-shop texture.
Use large 12-inch flour tortillas. Smaller tortillas will be difficult to roll with this much filling.
Do not overfill the burritos. A tightly rolled slightly smaller burrito eats better than a huge torn one.
Toast the assembled burrito seam-side down first. It seals the fold, melts the cheese, and gives the tortilla a lightly crisp exterior.
The foil rest matters. It helps the cheese fully melt, lets the burrito hold together, and gives the tortilla a better breakfast-shop texture.
Keep the hot sauce on the side if you want the tortilla to stay firmer. Drip it onto each bite while eating.
The guajillo chiles give the sauce body, color, and deeper red chile flavor without making it aggressively hot. The chile de arbol peppers provide the sharper heat.
Do not use scorched dried chiles. A few dark spots on roasted vegetables are good, but blackened dried chiles can make the sauce bitter.
If the chile soaking water tastes bitter, do not use it. Fresh hot water gives the sauce a cleaner finished flavor.
Simmering the blended hot sauce in oil makes it taste more finished, less raw, and more like a burrito-shop sauce.
For a milder hot sauce, remove the jalapeno seeds and use 1 dried chile de arbol pepper instead of 2.
For a hotter hot sauce, use 4 to 5 dried chile de arbol peppers or add 1 serrano.
The hot sauce should be loose enough to drip but not watery. Add chile soaking water or fresh hot water 1 tablespoon at a time only after blending or simmering.
These burritos are best eaten immediately after the foil rest, but leftovers can be wrapped tightly in foil and reheated in a 350 degrees F oven until hot.
title: Sausage, Egg, Potato, and Cheese Breakfast Burritos with Simmered Guajillo-Arbol Breakfast Drip Sauce

additionalTime: 2 to 8 hours steak marinade, 10 minutes chile soak, 10 minutes steak rest, plus 10 minutes potato steam-dry time
author: Robert Carr
description: Carne asada burritos with citrus-garlic marinated steak, savory Mexican-style rice, creamy pinto beans, extra-crispy seasoned potatoes, Oaxaca-Jack cheese, avocado crema, quick pickled onions, a toasted flour tortilla, and a smooth hot-oil-simmered roasted arbol-tomatillo drip hot sauce on the side.

groceryIngredients.item: flap steak, skirt steak, or flank steak
Oaxaca cheese or low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella

ingredients: 1 pound flap steak, skirt steak, or flank steak
1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the steak, or 3/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
3 tablespoons fresh orange juice, from about 1 orange
2 tablespoons fresh lime juice for the marinade
1 1/2 tablespoons avocado oil for the marinade and steak, divided
1 1/2 teaspoons minced chipotle pepper in adobo
3 cloves garlic for the marinade, finely grated or minced
1/2 teaspoon black pepper for the marinade
2 teaspoons fresh lime juice for finishing the steak
1 small pinch kosher salt for finishing the steak, only if needed
2 teaspoons avocado oil for the rice
1 tablespoon finely minced white onion for the rice
1 clove garlic for the rice, finely minced
2 teaspoons tomato paste for the rice
3/4 cup plus 2 tablespoons chicken broth for the rice
1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the rice, or 1/8 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1 tablespoon finely chopped cilantro for the rice
2 teaspoons fresh lime juice for the rice
1 15-ounce can pinto beans, drained but not rinsed
1/4 cup chicken broth or water for the beans
2 teaspoons avocado oil for the beans
1/4 small white onion for the beans, finely diced
1 clove garlic for the beans, minced
1/4 teaspoon black pepper for the beans
1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the beans, or 1/8 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
10 ounces Yukon Gold potatoes, cut into 1/2-inch cubes
2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt for parboiling the potatoes
1/8 teaspoon baking soda for parboiling the potatoes
1 1/2 tablespoons avocado oil for crisping the potatoes, divided
1/4 teaspoon smoked paprika for the potatoes
1/4 teaspoon garlic powder for the potatoes
1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the potatoes, or 1/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1/8 teaspoon black pepper for the potatoes
1 tablespoon fresh lime juice for the avocado crema
2 tablespoons cilantro for the avocado crema
1/2 small garlic clove for the avocado crema
1/8 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the avocado crema
3 tablespoons apple cider vinegar for the pickled onions
3 tablespoons water for the pickled onions
1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the pickled onions
2 ounces Oaxaca cheese, freshly shredded, or low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella
2 ounces Monterey Jack cheese, freshly shredded
1/4 small white onion for the hot sauce
3 unpeeled garlic cloves for the hot sauce
4 dried chile de arbol peppers, stems removed
1/4 cup hot water for soaking the toasted chiles
1/2 teaspoon tomato paste for the hot sauce
2 tablespoons apple cider vinegar for the hot sauce
1 tablespoon fresh lime juice for the hot sauce
1 to 3 tablespoons water for thinning the hot sauce
1 1/2 tablespoons avocado oil for the hot sauce
1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the hot sauce, or 1/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1/2 teaspoon black pepper for the hot sauce
instructions: Trim any large pieces of silver skin from the 1 pound flap steak, skirt steak, or flank steak, then pat the steak very dry with paper towels.
Season both sides of the steak with 1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, or 3/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt.

In a bowl or zip-top bag, mix 3 tablespoons fresh orange juice, 2 tablespoons fresh lime juice, 1 tablespoon avocado oil, 1 1/2 teaspoons Worcestershire sauce, 1/2 teaspoon low-sodium soy sauce, 1 1/2 teaspoons minced chipotle pepper in adobo, 3 grated or minced garlic cloves, 1/2 teaspoon cumin, 1/2 teaspoon Mexican oregano, 1/2 teaspoon black pepper, and 1/2 teaspoon brown sugar.
Add the salted steak to the marinade, turn until coated, cover, and refrigerate for at least 2 hours or up to 8 hours.

Make the quick pickled onions by combining 1/2 thinly sliced small red onion, 3 tablespoons apple cider vinegar, 3 tablespoons water, 1 1/2 teaspoons granulated sugar, and 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt in a bowl.
Stir the pickled onions well, press them down into the liquid, and let them sit while the rest of the recipe is prepared.

Make the roasted arbol-tomatillo drip hot sauce by placing 2 tomatillos, 1 Roma tomato, 2 jalapenos, 1/4 small white onion, and 3 unpeeled garlic cloves in a dry cast-iron skillet over medium-high heat.

Cook the hot sauce vegetables for 8 to 12 minutes, turning occasionally, until the tomatillos soften, the garlic skins are browned in spots, and the tomatoes, jalapenos, and onion are charred in places.
Remove the garlic cloves as soon as they are browned and softened, let them cool briefly, peel them, and transfer the peeled roasted garlic to a blender.
Transfer the roasted tomatillos, tomatoes, jalapenos, and onion to the blender.
Toast 4 dried chile de arbol peppers in the dry skillet for 15 to 30 seconds, just until fragrant and slightly darkened.
Transfer the toasted chiles to a small bowl, cover them with 1/4 cup hot water, and soak for 10 minutes so the hot sauce blends smoother and less papery.
Drain the soaked chiles, discard the soaking water, and transfer the chiles to the blender.

Add 1/2 teaspoon tomato paste, 2 tablespoons apple cider vinegar, 1 tablespoon fresh lime juice, 1 tablespoon water, 1/2 teaspoon honey, 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/2 teaspoon black pepper to the blender.
Blend the hot sauce until very smooth.
For a smoother drip sauce, pass the blended hot sauce through a fine-mesh strainer before simmering; for a more rustic sauce, leave it unstrained.
Heat 1 1/2 tablespoons avocado oil in a small saucepan over medium heat until shimmering.
Carefully pour the blended hot sauce into the hot oil; it may splatter at first.
Simmer the hot sauce over medium-low heat for 4 to 6 minutes, stirring often, until the raw vinegar edge rounds off, the color deepens slightly, and the oil looks integrated.
Add 1 to 2 tablespoons more water only if needed to make the hot sauce loose enough to drip from a spoon.
Taste the hot sauce and adjust with a small pinch of salt, a few drops of lime juice, or a few drops of honey only if needed.
Rinse 1/2 cup long-grain white rice under cool water until the water runs mostly clear, then drain well.
Heat 2 teaspoons avocado oil in a medium saucepan over medium heat.
Add the drained rice and toast for 2 to 3 minutes, stirring often, until the rice smells nutty and some grains turn lightly opaque.
Add 1 tablespoon finely minced white onion and 1 finely minced garlic clove, then cook for 30 seconds.
Add 2 teaspoons tomato paste and 1/4 teaspoon cumin, then stir for 30 seconds so the tomato paste darkens slightly.
Add 3/4 cup plus 2 tablespoons chicken broth, 1 bay leaf if using, and 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
Bring the rice to a boil, stir once, cover, reduce the heat to low, and cook for 15 minutes.
Turn off the heat and let the rice steam, covered and undisturbed, for 10 minutes.
Remove the bay leaf, fluff the rice, then fold in 1 tablespoon finely chopped cilantro and 2 teaspoons fresh lime juice.
Make the creamy pinto beans by heating 2 teaspoons avocado oil in a small saucepan over medium heat.
Add 1/4 small finely diced white onion and cook for 3 to 4 minutes, until softened.
Add 1 minced garlic clove, 1/2 teaspoon cumin, 1/4 teaspoon black pepper, and 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, then cook for 30 seconds.
Add 1 drained 15-ounce can pinto beans and 1/4 cup chicken broth or water.
Simmer the beans for 6 to 8 minutes, stirring often, until creamy but not soupy.
Mash about half of the beans against the side of the pan so the mixture becomes spreadable and can act as the burrito glue.
Make the crispy potatoes by placing 10 ounces 1/2-inch Yukon Gold potato cubes in a saucepan and covering them with cold water by 1 inch.
Add 2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt and 1/8 teaspoon baking soda to the potato water.
Bring the potatoes to a boil, then simmer for 5 to 6 minutes, until the outsides are slightly tender but the centers are not falling apart.
Drain the potatoes very well, return them to the empty warm pan, and shake the pan gently for 15 to 20 seconds to rough up the potato edges without breaking the cubes apart.
Spread the potatoes on a rimmed baking sheet and let them steam dry for 10 minutes.

Toss the dried potatoes with 1 1/2 tablespoons avocado oil, 1/4 teaspoon smoked paprika, 1/4 teaspoon garlic powder, 1/4 teaspoon cumin, 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/8 teaspoon black pepper.
Heat a cast-iron skillet over medium-high heat until hot.
Cook the potatoes in a single layer with space between pieces, working in batches only if your skillet is small.
Cook for 8 to 10 minutes, turning every few minutes, until crisp and browned on several sides.

Make the avocado crema by blending or mashing 1/2 avocado, 3 tablespoons sour cream, 1 tablespoon fresh lime juice, 2 tablespoons cilantro, 1/2 small garlic clove, and 1/8 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt until smooth.
Remove the steak from the marinade and scrape off any heavy garlic or herb clumps so they do not burn.
Pat the steak very dry with paper towels; this is important for browning instead of steaming.
Heat a cast-iron skillet or grill pan over medium-high to high heat until very hot.
Add up to 1 tablespoon avocado oil only if the pan looks dry.
Sear the steak in batches if needed so the pan stays very hot and the steak browns instead of steams.
Sear the steak for 3 to 5 minutes per side, depending on thickness, until deeply browned and the center reaches 130 to 135 degrees F for medium-rare or 140 to 145 degrees F for medium.
For USDA guidance, cook beef steaks to 145 degrees F with a 3-minute rest; for medium-rare preference, use fresh intact steak and cook to 130 to 135 degrees F if that is your desired doneness.
Transfer the steak to a cutting board and rest for 10 minutes.
Slice the rested steak thinly against the grain, then cut the slices into bite-size pieces so the burrito is easy to bite through.
Toss the sliced steak with any resting juices, 2 teaspoons fresh lime juice, and a small pinch of kosher salt only if the steak tastes like it needs it.
Mix 2 ounces freshly shredded Oaxaca cheese or low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella and 2 ounces freshly shredded Monterey Jack cheese in a bowl.
Warm each 12-inch flour tortilla in a dry skillet for 20 to 30 seconds per side, or until soft, flexible, and lightly toasted in spots.
Assemble 1 burrito at a time by placing a warm tortilla on a clean work surface.
Spread about 1/3 cup creamy pinto beans just below the center of the tortilla, leaving a wide border on the sides.
Add about 1/3 cup Mexican-style rice over the beans.
Add about 1/3 cup crispy potatoes over the rice.
Add half of the sliced carne asada over the potatoes.
Sprinkle 2 ounces Oaxaca-Jack cheese over the hot steak.
Add 1 to 2 tablespoons avocado crema and a small forkful of drained pickled onions.
Fold the left and right sides of the tortilla over the filling, then roll tightly from the bottom into a compact burrito.
Place the burrito seam-side down in a dry skillet over medium heat.
Toast the burrito for 1 to 2 minutes per side, starting with the seam side, until lightly crisp, sealed, and browned in spots.
Repeat with the remaining tortilla and fillings.
Serve each toasted burrito with the roasted arbol-tomatillo hot sauce on the side.
Drip a small spoonful of hot sauce onto each bite as you eat instead of pouring it into the burrito, which keeps the tortilla from getting soggy.
notes: Best steak choice: flap steak is the easiest high-reward option because it has strong beef flavor, good fat, and a forgiving texture. Skirt steak is excellent but can be chewier if sliced wrong. Flank steak works well but is leaner.

Do not marinate the steak overnight. The citrus is useful, but too much time in an acidic marinade can make the surface texture mushy. For skirt steak, stay closer to 2 to 4 hours; for flap or flank steak, 4 to 8 hours works well.
The marinade keeps Worcestershire as the main savory background note and uses only 1 teaspoon soy sauce so the steak tastes like carne asada instead of steakhouse beef.
The steak must be patted very dry before searing. Wet marinade left on the surface prevents browning and can make the steak taste steamed.
Sear the steak in batches if needed. Crowding the skillet traps steam and weakens the crust.
Slice the steak against the grain, then cut it into bite-size pieces. Long strips pull out of the burrito when you bite into it.
Tossing the sliced steak with its resting juices and a little lime wakes the steak back up after searing and resting.
The rice is intentionally more savory than plain cilantro-lime rice. Tomato paste, onion, garlic, broth, and cumin make it taste integrated with the burrito instead of like a separate side dish.
The beans should be creamy and spreadable, not loose. They act as a moisture barrier and help hold the burrito together.
This recipe intentionally makes extra rice and beans. Use only about 1/3 cup of each per burrito so the burritos roll tightly and do not become heavy or mushy.
The potatoes add crisp texture, absorb steak juices, and make the burrito more satisfying without turning it watery.
Baking soda and roughing up the potato edges help create a starchy exterior that browns and crisps harder.
Steam-drying the potatoes before crisping matters. Wet potatoes brown poorly and soften faster inside the burrito.
Cook the potatoes in a roomy single layer, or in 2 batches if your skillet is small. Crowded potatoes steam instead of crisp.

Freshly shredded Oaxaca and Monterey Jack melt better than pre-shredded cheese. If Oaxaca is hard to find, low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella is the best substitute because it gives a similar mild flavor and stretchy melt. Avoid fresh mozzarella here because it is too wet for a burrito.
Drain the pickled onions before adding them to the burrito. Their job is brightness and crunch, not extra liquid.
Use large 12-inch flour tortillas if possible. Smaller tortillas make overstuffed burritos that tear or cannot be rolled tightly.
Toast the assembled burrito seam-side down first. It seals the fold, improves texture, and gives the tortilla a lightly crisp exterior.
The hot sauce is intentionally served outside the burrito. Dripping it onto each bite gives heat, acid, and roasted chile flavor without soaking the tortilla.
Roasting the hot sauce garlic unpeeled lowers the risk of scorching the cloves and making the sauce bitter.
Soaking the toasted chile de arbol peppers before blending makes the hot sauce smoother, rounder, and less papery.

Simmering the blended hot sauce in avocado oil makes the roasted chile flavor rounder, softens the raw vinegar edge, and gives it a smooth texture that drips cleanly but still lightly coats each bite.
With a high-powered blender such as a Vitamix 5200, the sauce can be very smooth without straining, but straining gives the cleanest restaurant-style drip texture.
For a milder hot sauce, use 3 to 4 dried chile de arbol peppers instead of 8 and remove the jalapeno seeds before blending.
For a hotter sauce, use 10 to 12 dried chile de arbol peppers or add 1 serrano with the jalapenos.
The hot sauce should be loose enough to drip but not watery. Add water 1 tablespoon at a time only after blending.
For USDA guidance, cook beef steaks to 145 degrees F with a 3-minute rest. The medium-rare range is included for people who prefer that doneness when using fresh intact steak.
Eat these right after the final skillet toast.
servings: 2 large burritos, with extra rice and beans
title: Carne Asada Burritos with Crispy Potatoes and Roasted Arbol-Tomatillo Drip Hot Sauce
totalTime: 1 hour 55 minutes plus 2 to 8 hours steak marinade
